CZ_NAME: LEADVILLE VICINITY / LAKE COUNTY BELOW 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During late March, yet another deep upper level trough dug in across the western US. This, along with a strong southwesterly flow from the subtropical jet, brought in sufficient moisture and synoptic/orographic lift for another round of snowfall for parts of SE Colorado. Additionally, the strong synoptic forcing and convective nature of parts of this system did allow for the formation of one landspout tornado on March 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 7.5 inches of snow measured.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over the central and southern mountains of Colorado May 11th and 12th dropping as much as 18 inches of snow over the Mosquito and Ten Mile Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Here's a list of heavy snowfall reports from the 11th and 12th: Buckskin Joe SNOTEL 3 miles west-northwest of Alma an estimated 19 inches, Hoosier Pass SNOTEL 5 miles south-southwest of Blue River an estimated 18 inches Fremont Pass SNOTEL 1 mile northwest of Climax an estimated 16 inches, and a report from the public in Alma of 16 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep strong low pressure system moved down the west coast and through the Great Basin and south of the Four Corners region, providing very cold air and ample lift to result in significant snowfall for the mountains and valleys of southeast Utah and southwest Colorado. Snow bands also set up across southeast Utah allowing for some areas to see locally higher amounts. While the central Colorado mountains also saw some snow, totals were not nearly as much as the southern mountains. A much colder, arctic air mass was left in this system's wake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 15 inches of snow fell across the area with 14.6 inches at Pagosa Springs 0.2 N.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of disturbances associated with a strong jet across the north tracked across northwest Colorado, producing moderate snowfall and strong winds favoring the northwest Colorado mountains and Upper Yampa River Basin. This strong jet across the north was associated with a deep trough that allowed an arctic air mass to drop southward across much of Colorado and areas east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area, with locally higher amount up to 2 feet at Tower, which could be wind affected. Winds gusted 55 to 60 mph at Storm Peak Lab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Trapped low level moisture under strong, northwesterly flow allowed for the development of low stratus and dense fog in valleys across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed in the Upper Gunnison River Valley via satellite imagery and AWOS reports. The Gunnison Airport AWOS reported visibilities reduced to less than 1/2 mile, and less than 1/4 mile at times during the 5 hour period of the Advisory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of disturbances quickly dropped into the Four Corners region, providing moderate snowfall and blowing snow due to strong winds favoring the southern and central Colorado mountains. The northern Park range and Upper Yampa River Basin continued to see persistent snowfall under this arctic air mass due to a favored northwest orographic flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds generally gusted in the 20s with a peak gust of 77 mph detected at Storm Peak Lab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent northwest flow due to a strong jet over the region along with remnants of an atmospheric river and an arctic air mass in place, resulted in another round of moderate to heavy snowfall and strong winds favoring the northern and central mountains of western Colorado. The San Juan Mountains still saw some moderate snowfall but to a lesser extent than the northern and central mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 18 inches of snow fell across the area with 18 inches at Tower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately strong atmospheric river coming in from the west in conjunction with a low pressure system dropping southward out of the Pacific Northwest tracked along a strong northwest to southeast oriented jet. This led to significant snowfall across all mountain ranges of western Colorado with lesser amounts across the La Sals in southeast Utah. Some lower northwest and west-central higher valleys even saw moderate snowfall. Strong winds in the higher elevations continued to bring blowing and drifting snow as well as whiteout conditions and road closures to most mountain passes. A strong arctic front dropped in from the north late in the storms period, leading to some of the whiteout concerns and bringing continued snow in the Uncompahgre Gorge and north facing slopes. Snowfall was limited in the lower valleys due to cold arctic air being scoured out from the winds leading up to this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 24 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amount of 31.5 inches at Ripple Creek.

Some lower northwest and west-central higher valleys even saw moderate snowfall. Strong winds in the higher elevations continued to bring blowing and drifting snow as well as whiteout conditions and road closures to most mountain passes. A strong arctic front dropped in from the north late in the storms period, leading to some of the whiteout concerns and bringing continued snow in the Uncompahgre Gorge and north facing slopes. Snowfall was limited in the lower valleys due to cold arctic air being scoured out from the winds leading up to this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with 20.3 inches at Oak Creek 0.4 W. Winds gusted in the 20s and 30s with a peak wind gust of 46 mph at Steamboat Springs Airport.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved from northwest to southeast across all of eastern Utah and western Colorado, with significant banding and a long lived snow squall that accompanied the cold front. This resulted in strong winds with gusts 45 to 55 mph in the valleys along the boundary with gusts 60 to 80 mph in the mountains.  Low visibilities and whiteout conditions also occurred at times along this frontal boundary due to falling and blowing snow.  Accumulations in the lower elevations were 2 inches or less in spots due to the brief nature of the snowfall along the front as well as milder temperatures, but the impacts due to the snow squalls were felt across much of the region as temperatures plummeted behind it. The San Juan mountains received the heaviest snow with the central Colorado mountains receiving moderate snowfall accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally trace to 2 inches of snow fell with 2 inches reported at Northdale COOP. Winds gusted in the 25 to 35 mph range but peaked 40 to 43 mph at the Cortez Municipal Airport on both February 26 and 27. The Cortez Municipal Airport reported snow and visibility down to 0.5 miles during the snow squall event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southwesterly jet aloft ahead of a deep Pacific low pressure system moved over eastern Utah and western Colorado starting early on the 26th. As the day progressed, these strong winds began to mix down, bringing strong gusts of 40-50 mph across much of the area. These winds maximized ahead of a potent cold front that begin impacting northern portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado late on the 26th, and finally moved south of the Four Corners around midday on the 27th. During this period, gusts above 58 mph in the valleys and above 75 mph in the mountains were recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts in the range of 70-80 mph were recorded at the higher elevations of Colorado Zone 012, including a peak gust of 76 mph at the Monarch Pass AWOS, KMYP. The peak gust occurred around 12:30 pm on the 26th, but gusts into the 70s continued through mid-morning on the 27th.

A storm system produced a period of heavy snow and strong winds in the northern mountain. Storm totals ranged from 10 to nearly 20 inches.  The Elliot Ridge SNOTEL site west of Green Mountain Reservoir received the heaviest snowfall, with an estimated 19.5 inches. Peak wind gusts generally ranged from 45 to 60 mph above timberline, but there was a peak wind gust to 75 mph recorded atop Peak 8 at Breckenridge Ski Area.  Snow and blowing snow resulted in a number of accidents along the Interstate 70 corridor, which resulted in occasional road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals ranged from 3 to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front and upper level trough moved through the area from northwest to southeast, bringing moderate snow to the mountains of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The La Sal Mountains in Southeast Utah and the Northwest San Juan Mountains were favored for the heaviest snowfall accumulations due to favored northerly orographics and colder air in place. Stronger winds were seen above 12,000 feet MSL across the West Elk Mountains and San Juan Mountains of Western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Douglas Pass received 7 inches of snow. Winds gusted in the 50 to 60 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The closed low dug down in a deep trough across the Rocky Mountains and SE Colorado. This brough more widespread heavy snow and strong winds to our forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a combination of COOP observed snowfall, visibility less than one quarter mile, heavy snow, and wind gusting up to 30 mph for 3 consecutive hours.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system produced a period of heavy snow and strong winds in the northern mountain. Storm totals ranged from 10 to nearly 20 inches.  The Elliot Ridge SNOTEL site west of Green Mountain Reservoir received the heaviest snowfall, with an estimated 19.5 inches. Peak wind gusts generally ranged from 45 to 60 mph above timberline, but there was a peak wind gust to 75 mph recorded atop Peak 8 at Breckenridge Ski Area.  Snow and blowing snow resulted in a number of accidents along the Interstate 70 corridor, which resulted in occasional road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  16.9 inches near Cameron Pass and 6 miles west of Arapahoe Peak, 15.6 inches near Mount Audubon, and 14.3 inches, 9 miles south-southeast of Spicer.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm struck the Front Range of Colorado March 13th and 14th, 2024, producing extremely heavy snowfall, difficult if not impossible travel conditions, and scattered power outages. While precipitation started in the form of snow in the mountains and foothills on March 13, it started as rain across the I-25 Corridor and plains. That rain mixed with and changed to a heavy, wet snow across the Denver and Boulder metro areas during the late evening of the 13th. However, areas roughly along and east of a line from Fort Collins to Denver International Airport remained mostly rain or a rain snow mix until the early morning hours of March 14th. Some locations farther east like Greeley, Fort Morgan, and Limon didn���t changeover until late on the 14th.  Total precipitation amounts in those areas and farther east across the plains were also much lighter.   While there was a slight lull in the storm and snowfall rates on the morning of the 14th, snowfall rates increased again during the afternoon and early evening hours.  The snow then tapered off during the late evening and overnight hours, and ended in the early morning of the 15th.  ||Difficult to impossible travel conditions started in the foothills shortly after the onset of heavy snow on the 13th. I-70 was closed, and dozens of vehicles became stuck and/or were abandoned.  A ski bus full of 50 women ran out of food and water after being stuck for 16 hours. The depth of snow became so great that travel became impossible on unplowed roads for even high clearance or four-wheel drive vehicles. Some residents in the foothills were trapped for days in their homes.  Also, numerous tree limbs snapped under the weight of snow, leading to scattered power outages. An estimated 113,000 customers were without power at some point during the storm, and that was before trees had even begun to leaf out for the spring!  An estimated 800 flights were cancelled at Denver International Airport leading up to the snow, although fortunately the heaviest accumulations and impacts stayed just west of the airport. Widespread school closures occurred on the 14th and 15th from the Denver metro area into the foothills.  ||Storm total snow accumulations in the feet were observed across the foothills and east facing slopes of the Front Range mountains.  Most areas above 6,000 feet westward to the crest of the Front Range (Medicine Bow Range, Rocky Mountain National Park, Indian Peaks and Mosquito Range) saw at least two feet of snow, while most of the foothills from Estes Park southward through Boulder, Gilpin, Clear Creek, and Jefferson Counties received in excess of three feet! A few spots saw from four to as much as five feet between Ward and Blackhawk, and also southeast of Idaho Springs (Beaver Brook neighborhood).   The top five storm totals included: 61.5 inches in Aspen Springs, 61 inches, 5.4 miles northwest of Evergreen, 57.0 inches, 1 mile west of Aspen Springs, 54.4 inches near Rollinsville, and 53.7 inches, with 4.7 miles south-southeast of Idaho Springs.  Colorado issued emergency disaster declarations for Clear Creek and Gilpin counties, due to the heavy amounts of snow, along with stranded motorists and residents.  The main closures along Interstate 70 included:  I-70 eastbound near Silver Plume, I-70 westbound in Georgetown, I-70 in both directions near Genesee, I-70 in both directions near Morrison, I-70 eastbound near East Frisco and Silverthorne, I-70 eastbound near Silverthorne and Loveland Pass.  Nearly two dozen trucks were stuck along US 285, southwest of Denver, which resulted in its closure as well.||In the Denver metro area and Boulder, accumulations mostly ranged between 10 and 18 inches, with nearly two feet of snow  from the southwestern sections of Boulder into the Golden area, extending southward through the far southern Denver suburbs into Douglas county and Castle Rock.  There was a sharp gradient of snowfall to the east, with the far northeast sections of Denver - including DIA - receiving only 5 to 10 inches of snow. The official total at DIA was 5.7 inches. Similar amounts were seen farther north toward Firestone, Longmont, and the northeastern side of Fort Collins. At the National Weather Service Office in Boulder, 20.5 inches of snow was observed while only an inch or two of snow accumulated around Greeley, Fort Morgan, and Limon.  On March 13th, 0.80 of rainfall was measured at Denver International Airport, which broke the daily record.  The 2-day total, for March 13-14th, was 1.46 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  53 inches, 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland, 46 inches at Eldora Ski Area, and 40 inches near Pennock Pass.

The top five storm totals included: 61.5 inches in Aspen Springs, 61 inches, 5.4 miles northwest of Evergreen, 57.0 inches, 1 mile west of Aspen Springs, 54.4 inches near Rollinsville, and 53.7 inches, with 4.7 miles south-southeast of Idaho Springs.  Colorado issued emergency disaster declarations for Clear Creek and Gilpin counties, due to the heavy amounts of snow, along with stranded motorists and residents.  The main closures along Interstate 70 included:  I-70 eastbound near Silver Plume, I-70 westbound in Georgetown, I-70 in both directions near Genesee, I-70 in both directions near Morrison, I-70 eastbound near East Frisco and Silverthorne, I-70 eastbound near Silverthorne and Loveland Pass.  Nearly two dozen trucks were stuck along US 285, southwest of Denver, which resulted in its closure as well.||In the Denver metro area and Boulder, accumulations mostly ranged between 10 and 18 inches, with nearly two feet of snow  from the southwestern sections of Boulder into the Golden area, extending southward through the far southern Denver suburbs into Douglas county and Castle Rock.  There was a sharp gradient of snowfall to the east, with the far northeast sections of Denver - including DIA - receiving only 5 to 10 inches of snow. The official total at DIA was 5.7 inches. Similar amounts were seen farther north toward Firestone, Longmont, and the northeastern side of Fort Collins. At the National Weather Service Office in Boulder, 20.5 inches of snow was observed while only an inch or two of snow accumulated around Greeley, Fort Morgan, and Limon.  On March 13th, 0.80 of rainfall was measured at Denver International Airport, which broke the daily record.  The 2-day total, for March 13-14th, was 1.46 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  26.0 inches near Lakewood, 23.0 inches near Louisville, 22.0 inches near Lafayette,  21.3 inches near Superior, 21.0 inches near Arvada, Broomfield and Lakewood; and 20.5 inches at the National Weather Service in Boulder.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During late March, yet another deep upper level trough dug in across the western US. This, along with a strong southwesterly flow from the subtropical jet, brought in sufficient moisture and synoptic/orographic lift for another round of snowfall for parts of SE Colorado. Additionally, the strong synoptic forcing and convective nature of parts of this system did allow for the formation of one landspout tornado on March 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 5.0 inches of snow measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low moved through Eastern Utah and settled south of the Four Corners region where it stalled and became cut off. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds occurred ahead of a strong colf front that resulted in a high wind event and snow levels dropping to all valley floors after frontal passage. Signficant moisture supplied by an Atmospheric River fueled this storm system as it dropped in from the Intermountain West and brought feet of snow across the northern and central mountains of northeast Utah and western Colorado, with much less across southwest Colorado. Some lower northern and central valleys of western Colorado also saw significant snow with near white out conditions and banded snowfall or squall conditions at times. Winds continued to be strong throughout this event with gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range in the valleys and 60 to 80 mph range in the mountains on average.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with 13.8 inches reported at Hayden 1.6 SW. Wind gusts were in the 40 to 55 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system produced a period of moderate to heavy snowfall over the northeast plains of Colorado, and impacted Logan, Phillips, Sedgwick and Washington counties.  The heavy snow forced safety closures for portions of Interstate 76 and US-34 as the roadways became icy and snowpacked.  Colorado State Patrol reported that multiple drivers had slid off the road in Washington County. Storm totals ranged from 5 to 8 inches.  One observer measured 9.7 inches of snowfall near Fleming.  In and near the Front Range Mountains and Foothills, storm totals ranged from 3 to 8 inches.  Roads were icy and slick at times, with poor visibility from patchy dense fog and snow.  On the morning of the 8th, black ice that formed from melting snow overnight lead to several spinouts and accidents.  The hazardous road conditions forced the closure of State Highway 93 in Jefferson County, which impacted the morning commute from Golden to Boulder.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured 9.7 inches of snowfall, 3 miles south-southwest of Fleming.

CZ_NAME: PARADOX VALLEY / LOWER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure trough moved into the Pacific Northwest and dropped south through the Great Basin in the days leading up to March 1st. At the base of this trough, a 160 knot jet aloft tracked overhead of eastern Utah and western Colorado beginning on the 1st, with daytime heating starting to mix down these much stronger southwest winds. Winds peaked during the late morning and afternoon hours on the 2nd as the pressure gradient through the atmosphere tightened up ahead of a strong cold front. Strongest winds were observed just prior to the cold front's passage, and led to numerous reports of power outages, downed trees, and even a few small brush fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While gusts across Colorado Zone 020 were generally less than criteria needed for this to be a High Wind Event, with gusts ranging from 45-55 mph, numerous power outages were reported via the local power provider, San Miguel Power, across the Paradox Valley, Disappointment Valley, and western Montrose County region, including the town of Norwood.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system produced a period of moderate to heavy snowfall over the northeast plains of Colorado, and impacted Logan, Phillips, Sedgwick and Washington counties.  The heavy snow forced safety closures for portions of Interstate 76 and US-34 as the roadways became icy and snowpacked.  Colorado State Patrol reported that multiple drivers had slid off the road in Washington County. Storm totals ranged from 5 to 8 inches.  One observer measured 9.7 inches of snowfall near Fleming.  In and near the Front Range Mountains and Foothills, storm totals ranged from 3 to 8 inches.  Roads were icy and slick at times, with poor visibility from patchy dense fog and snow.  On the morning of the 8th, black ice that formed from melting snow overnight lead to several spinouts and accidents.  The hazardous road conditions forced the closure of State Highway 93 in Jefferson County, which impacted the morning commute from Golden to Boulder.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies in the wake of a trough combined with moisture trapped in the Grand Valley to produce a small but dense area of fog along the Colorado River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patches of dense fog were reported across the Grand Valley, with the KGJT ASOS reporting visibilities between 1/2 SM and 1/4 SM. In addition, webcam imagery and numerous public reports indicated areas with visibility less than 100 yards in and around the Colorado River near Fruita and the Redlands area of Grand Junction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On a day with Considerable (3/5) avalanche danger, per the Colorado Avalanche Information Center, a group backcountry snowmobilers triggered an avalanche that seriously injured one of their party members.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Avalanche danger in the area rated Considerable (3/5) at all elevations on the day of the incident. A group of 6 backcountry snowmobilers unintentionally and remotely triggered an avalanche on the morning of the 6th at around 9:30 AM. The 2nd rider in the group was caught in the avalanche and buried. When the 2nd rider was extricated, he was unconscious and not breathing and CPR was performed. He did regain a pulse and began breathing on his own at the scene, but did not regain consciousness for several hours. He was snowmobiled out and eventually transferred to the hospital where he made full recovery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the afternoon of the 24th, a strong low pressure system moved across the region, causing thunderstorms in the afternoon hours. As night fell, temperatures dropped and snow began falling as winds increased. A gust of 57 mph was recorded in Cheyenne county Colorado. These winds and heavy snow caused brief blizzard conditions for a cumulative 2 hours, according to the Burlington ASOS. Due to the warm ground temperatures, only 2 inches of snow was reported in eastern Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of snow fell across the county as winds gusted up to 57 mph. Despite heavy, blowing snow causing brief blizzard conditions, no accumulating snow was reported due to warm ground temperatures. Key impacts: Reduction in visibility and slick roads causing hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure trough moved into the Pacific Northwest and dropped south through the Great Basin in the days leading up to March 1st. At the base of this trough, a 160 knot jet aloft tracked overhead of eastern Utah and western Colorado beginning on the 1st, with daytime heating starting to mix down these much stronger southwest winds. Winds peaked during the late morning and afternoon hours on the 2nd as the pressure gradient through the atmosphere tightened up ahead of a strong cold front. Strongest winds were observed just prior to the cold front's passage, and led to numerous reports of power outages, downed trees, and even a few small brush fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous mesonet sites across Colorado Zone 003 reported wind gusts above high wind thresholds during the day on March 2nd. The peak gust of 83 mph was recorded at both the mesonet site 1W Douglas Pass and the site 139s034 Douglass Pass between 1200 and 1500. Other recorded gusts in the zone ranged from 45 mph to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread area of snow moved over portions of Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties producing blizzard conditions up to four and a half hours and up to two inches of snow. Winds gusted up to 60 mph across the two counties, with the peak gust and snow amount observed in Burlington, Colorado. Highway 385 from Cheyenne Wells to Burlington, as well as Interstate 70 from Burlington to Kansas, had to be closed for a few hours due to the hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread area of snow moved over the eastern three-quarters of Kit Carson County producing four and a half hours of blizzard conditions. Conditions were mainly observed at the Burlington ASOS (KITR) with gusts up to 59 mph and visibility of less than one-quarter mile observed. High winds gusting 58 to 59 mph occurred sporadically over a duration of two hours from 11:53 AM to 1:53 PM MST over the Burlington ASOS. Total snowfall ranged from a trace over the western portions of the county, such as Flagler, to two inches in Burlington. Interstate 70 had to be closed for a few hours from Burlington to the Kansas state line due to road conditions.||Key impacts: limited visibility, snow packed roads, blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately strong atmospheric river coming in from the west in conjunction with a low pressure system dropping southward out of the Pacific Northwest tracked along a strong northwest to southeast oriented jet. This led to significant snowfall across all mountain ranges of western Colorado with lesser amounts across the La Sals in southeast Utah. Some lower northwest and west-central higher valleys even saw moderate snowfall. Strong winds in the higher elevations continued to bring blowing and drifting snow as well as whiteout conditions and road closures to most mountain passes. A strong arctic front dropped in from the north late in the storms period, leading to some of the whiteout concerns and bringing continued snow in the Uncompahgre Gorge and north facing slopes. Snowfall was limited in the lower valleys due to cold arctic air being scoured out from the winds leading up to this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amount of 9 inches at Hayden. Winds gusted 30 to 44 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low moved into the Four Corners region, producing favorable lift for snowfall in the Southwest Colorado mountains and valleys. Lingering northwest flow behind the system favored continued snowfall for the Uncompahgre Gorge in the Northwest San Juan Mountains as well as upslope snowfall in the Elkhead and Park Mountains and Upper Yampa River Basin well after the low exited to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 15 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 18 inches at Rabbit Ears Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough moved from the Pacific Northwest across northwest Colorado, along with remnants of an atmospheric river and produced heavy snowfall for mainly the northern and central Colorado mountains and moderate snow for the higher northern and central valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 9 inches of snow fell across the eastern side of the area near Hayden with 1 to 4 inches on the western side. Winds gusted in the 30 to 40 mph range at various sites throughout the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of disturbances quickly dropped into the Four Corners region, providing moderate snowfall and blowing snow due to strong winds favoring the southern and central Colorado mountains. The northern Park range and Upper Yampa River Basin continued to see persistent snowfall under this arctic air mass due to a favored northwest orographic flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area, mainly around the town of Steamboat Springs. 1 to 2 inches fell to the north and southern parts of the zone.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep strong low pressure system moved down the west coast and through the Great Basin and south of the Four Corners region, providing very cold air and ample lift to result in significant snowfall for the mountains and valleys of southeast Utah and southwest Colorado. Snow bands also set up across southeast Utah allowing for some areas to see locally higher amounts. While the central Colorado mountains also saw some snow, totals were not nearly as much as the southern mountains. A much colder, arctic air mass was left in this system's wake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 7 inches of snow fell across much of the area with locally higher amount of 15 inches at Dove Creek 12.4 SSW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the northern portions of northeast Utah and northwest Colorado and stalled along I-70, resulting in moderate snowfall and strong winds across the Eastern Uinta Mountains of Northeast Utah, as well as all mountain ranges of Northwest Colorado and West-Central Colorado. A low pressure trough also moved through the area and interacted with the cold front, resulting in locally higher amounts due to banding along the front, even into some higher northwest Colorado valleys. Milder temperatures resulted in very little snow in the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 11 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 13 inches of snow at Ripple Creek. Peak wind gusts reached 47 mph at Dead Horse RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Pacific storms in early to mid January brought heavy snow to the San Juan Mountains in Southwest Colorado resulting in a dangerously unstable snowpack.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A backcountry skier, who was alone, triggered an avalanche in Waterfall Canyon, south of the town of Ophir. Avalanche danger was rated as moderate (2/5) to Considerable (3/5) on the day. The skier was caught, managed to self extricate, but succumbed to injury and exposure roughly 500-600 feet below the avalanche. The avalanche occurred on a northwest facing slope at an elevation of 10,500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late morning of the 18th, a low pressure system swept through East Central Colorado. This system caused gusts up to 59 MPH and blowing snow that reduced visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the evening of the 12th, a strong cold front moved southward over the area. Behind it,  an arctic airmass caused -25F wind chills to occur early in the morning of the 13th. This airmass remained until the 16th, as did cold temperatures and wind chills. The coldest wind chill recorded in East Central Colorado during the event was -38F in Yuma County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the airport in Burlington, CO report reported -25F wind chills starting at 1245 AM on the 13th. There were brief times during the day time hours that wind chills warmed above -25F, generally due to the ASOS reporting calm winds. Overnight on the 15th, an additional cold front moved through and the ASOS reported the coldest reading of -37F wind chills.||Key impacts: frostbite, hypothermia.

High winds develop over the mountains and foothills of Grand and Boulder counties.  The gusts were localized but intense.  Peak gusts included 83 mph near Pinecliffe and 82 mph near Berthoud Pass.

Heavy snow and strong winds developed in the north-central mountains, with storm totals ranging from 1 to 2 feet.  The highest storm totals occurred in the mountains north of Interstate 70.  In the Front Range Foothills, storm totals ranged anywhere from 4 to 16.5 inches.  The higher amounts were more localized and focused in the foothills of Boulder and northern Jefferson counties. CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration mountain snowfall event occurred over the course of the Martin Luther King weekend, with several feet of snow. Most SNOTEL sites picked up an estimated 2 to 4 feet of snow.  The wind remain strong and gusty through the period, with the strongest gusts on the 13th and 15th, most intense above timberline.   A peak gust to 119 mph occurred at Peak 8 at Breckenridge on the 13th.  Numerous road closures were implemented over the course of the weekend, primarily on the 15th with the combination of snow and very strong winds.  A minor avalanche on Berthoud Pass closed US 40 during the afternoon of the 14th, and the closure continued  into the morning of the 16th.  Ten vehicles were reportedly caught in the first slide; no injuries were reported.  Road closures included:  US 40 from Steamboat Springs to Kremmling, in addition to the Berthoud Pass closure; CO-125 from Granby to the Wyoming Border, Interstate 70 from Vail to Copper Mountain; Silverthorne to the Eisenhower-Johnson Tunnel, and some stretches of I-70 east of the tunnel.  There were substantial impacts to holiday ski traffic.  At one point, every state and US highway in Jackson County was closed.  A few spots in the mountains were also impacted by bitter cold including a low temperatures of -34F in Hebron, and -33F in Grand Lake.| |Even in the mountain valleys, snow and blowing snow where gusts in the 50-70 mph range were more common.  The combination of strong winds and freshly fallen snow culminated in localized ground blizzards along State Highway 93 from Golden north to State Highway 128 in Jefferson County.  On the 15th, near whiteout conditions from localized ground blizzards made for a difficult commute from Golden to Boulder through much of the day.  US 285 over Kenosha Pass also experienced significant blowing snow and poor visibility. ||Some of the more impressive storm totals included:  51 inches near Green Mountain Reservoir and 4 miles southeast of Mt Zirkel; 49 inches at Rabbit Ears Pass, 45 inches near Winter Park, 42 inches at Mt Audubon and Arapahoe Peak; 40.5 inches, 9 miles southeast of Spicer, and 39 inches, 11 miles south of Rabbit Ears Pass.  Other peak wind gusts included:  114 mph near Copper Mountain, 112 mph near Berthoud Pass,  82 mph at Dakota, 81 mph at Red Cliff Pass	81 and 80 mph, 8 miles southwest of Tabernash.  ||Across the plains, a few rounds of light snow were accompanied by below zero temperatures and dangerous wind chills.  Some MLK Day events were either canceled or shortened.  Total snowfall accumulations across the urban corridor and northeast were light, with storm totals generally less than 6 inches, but the icy conditions did cause some travel impacts.  The coldest temperatures during this evening occurred on the 15th and the morning of the 16th.  Denver plummeted to -19F briefly on the evening of the 15th, nearly tying the record low for the day.  The temperatures dropped to -19F on the morning of the 16th which tied the record low for the date.    Some of the plains locations dropped down to -30F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  42 inches near Mt Audubon and Arapahoe Peak, 40.5 inches, 9 miles south-southeast of Spicer, 36 inches near Bear Lake and Brainard Lake, 30 inches near Longs Peak, 28.5 inches near Trail Ridge, 24 inches, 9 miles south-southeast of Gould, 22.5 inches Kinikinik, 21 inches near Pingree Park and 18 inches near Cameron Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration mountain snowfall event occurred over the course of the Martin Luther King weekend, with several feet of snow. Most SNOTEL sites picked up an estimated 2 to 4 feet of snow.  The wind remain strong and gusty through the period, with the strongest gusts on the 13th and 15th, most intense above timberline.   A peak gust to 119 mph occurred at Peak 8 at Breckenridge on the 13th.  Numerous road closures were implemented over the course of the weekend, primarily on the 15th with the combination of snow and very strong winds.  A minor avalanche on Berthoud Pass closed US 40 during the afternoon of the 14th, and the closure continued  into the morning of the 16th.  Ten vehicles were reportedly caught in the first slide; no injuries were reported.  Road closures included:  US 40 from Steamboat Springs to Kremmling, in addition to the Berthoud Pass closure; CO-125 from Granby to the Wyoming Border, Interstate 70 from Vail to Copper Mountain; Silverthorne to the Eisenhower-Johnson Tunnel, and some stretches of I-70 east of the tunnel.  There were substantial impacts to holiday ski traffic.  At one point, every state and US highway in Jackson County was closed.  A few spots in the mountains were also impacted by bitter cold including a low temperatures of -34F in Hebron, and -33F in Grand Lake.| |Even in the mountain valleys, snow and blowing snow where gusts in the 50-70 mph range were more common.  The combination of strong winds and freshly fallen snow culminated in localized ground blizzards along State Highway 93 from Golden north to State Highway 128 in Jefferson County.  On the 15th, near whiteout conditions from localized ground blizzards made for a difficult commute from Golden to Boulder through much of the day.  US 285 over Kenosha Pass also experienced significant blowing snow and poor visibility. ||Some of the more impressive storm totals included:  51 inches near Green Mountain Reservoir and 4 miles southeast of Mt Zirkel; 49 inches at Rabbit Ears Pass, 45 inches near Winter Park, 42 inches at Mt Audubon and Arapahoe Peak; 40.5 inches, 9 miles southeast of Spicer, and 39 inches, 11 miles south of Rabbit Ears Pass.  Other peak wind gusts included:  114 mph near Copper Mountain, 112 mph near Berthoud Pass,  82 mph at Dakota, 81 mph at Red Cliff Pass	81 and 80 mph, 8 miles southwest of Tabernash.  ||Across the plains, a few rounds of light snow were accompanied by below zero temperatures and dangerous wind chills.  Some MLK Day events were either canceled or shortened.  Total snowfall accumulations across the urban corridor and northeast were light, with storm totals generally less than 6 inches, but the icy conditions did cause some travel impacts.  The coldest temperatures during this evening occurred on the 15th and the morning of the 16th.  Denver plummeted to -19F briefly on the evening of the 15th, nearly tying the record low for the day.  The temperatures dropped to -19F on the morning of the 16th which tied the record low for the date.    Some of the plains locations dropped down to -30F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind chill temperature dropped as low as -48F.  The low temperature dropped to -30F on the morning of the 16th, establishing a new all-time record for the station since records began in 1948.  The minimum temperatures on the 15th dropped to -28F.

CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy wet snow occurred in and near the Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide.  Storm totals ranged from 8 to nearly 20 inches in those areas, including the western and southern Denver suburbs.  Further north and east, storm totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches in Fort Collins/Loveland, as well as eastern areas of metro Denver.  Denver International Airport recorded 0.72 of precipitation on the 3rd, which tied this storm for the 3rd wettest for the month of February, going back to 1872.  Numerous roads and highways from Denver west into the foothills were closed including Interstate 70, US 40, US 285 and C-470.  Numerous vehicles became stuck in the heavy wet snowfall, with rates up to 2 inches per hour making the roadways impassable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals ranged from 1 to 4 inches around Fort Collins and Loveland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved from northwest to southeast across all of eastern Utah and western Colorado, with significant banding and a long lived snow squall that accompanied the cold front. This resulted in strong winds with gusts 45 to 55 mph in the valleys along the boundary with gusts 60 to 80 mph in the mountains.  Low visibilities and whiteout conditions also occurred at times along this frontal boundary due to falling and blowing snow.  Accumulations in the lower elevations were 2 inches or less in spots due to the brief nature of the snowfall along the front as well as milder temperatures, but the impacts due to the snow squalls were felt across much of the region as temperatures plummeted behind it. The San Juan mountains received the heaviest snow with the central Colorado mountains receiving moderate snowfall accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 6.5 inches reported at the Craig and Meeker COOP sites. Wind gusts were in the 35 to 45 mph range on average with a peak gust of 48 mph at the Meeker Coulter Field Airport.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving storm system dropped out of the northwest and moved across northeast Colorado.  The storm system brought period of light to moderate snowfall to the region along with areas of freezing drizzle which impacted foothills and urban corridor in the late afternoon and evening period.  The storm system dropped a quick 2 to 5.5 inches of snowfall across the area.  The highest amounts occurred around Boulder.  Areas of freezing drizzle ahead of the system produced black ice which resulted in several accidents along Interstate 76 in southeast Weld and Morgan counties as well areas east and south of Denver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large snow band moved across portions of the Front Range Urban Corridor and eastern Colorado plains which significantly impacted the morning commute.  The brief burst of snow produced up to 2 inches of snow in one hour.  Strong winds also accompanied the cold front as the snow squall moved across the area.  A peak wind gust to 58 mph was observed at Denver International Airport from the northeast.  Several road closures occurred due to numerous accidents caused by slick roads and poor visibilities. US 285 from southwest Denver to Bailey, SH 93 and US 36, I-25, I-70, I-76 were all impacted.  Denver International Airport implemented a ground stop for nearly 90 minutes. Over 450 flights were delayed and several others were diverted to other airports.  A section of SH 14 in northeast Weld County was also impacted by a snow squall and well as I-76 around Sterling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall impacted US 285 from southwest of Denver to Bailey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southwesterly jet aloft ahead of a deep Pacific low pressure system moved over eastern Utah and western Colorado starting early on the 26th. As the day progressed, these strong winds began to mix down, bringing strong gusts of 40-50 mph across much of the area. These winds maximized ahead of a potent cold front that begin impacting northern portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado late on the 26th, and finally moved south of the Four Corners around midday on the 27th. During this period, gusts above 58 mph in the valleys and above 75 mph in the mountains were recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts ranging from 60-80 mph were recorded at several higher elevation sites across Colorado Zone 004, beginning around noon on the 26th and coming to an end with the passage of a cold front on the morning of the 27th. The peak gust of 79 mph was recorded around 4AM at the STORM 4.3 E Steamboat Springs Mesonet station.

This resulted in strong winds with gusts 45 to 55 mph in the valleys along the boundary with gusts 60 to 80 mph in the mountains.  Low visibilities and whiteout conditions also occurred at times along this frontal boundary due to falling and blowing snow.  Accumulations in the lower elevations were 2 inches or less in spots due to the brief nature of the snowfall along the front as well as milder temperatures, but the impacts due to the snow squalls were felt across much of the region as temperatures plummeted behind it. The San Juan mountains received the heaviest snow with the central Colorado mountains receiving moderate snowfall accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 7 inches reported at Skyway that was wind affected. Wind gusts peaked to 38 mph at Collbran during snow squalls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving storm system dropped out of the northwest and moved across northeast Colorado.  The storm system brought period of light to moderate snowfall to the region along with areas of freezing drizzle which impacted foothills and urban corridor in the late afternoon and evening period.  The storm system dropped a quick 2 to 5.5 inches of snowfall across the area.  The highest amounts occurred around Boulder.  Areas of freezing drizzle ahead of the system produced black ice which resulted in several accidents along Interstate 76 in southeast Weld and Morgan counties as well areas east and south of Denver.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy wet snow occurred in and near the Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide.  Storm totals ranged from 8 to nearly 20 inches in those areas, including the western and southern Denver suburbs.  Further north and east, storm totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches in Fort Collins/Loveland, as well as eastern areas of metro Denver.  Denver International Airport recorded 0.72 of precipitation on the 3rd, which tied this storm for the 3rd wettest for the month of February, going back to 1872.  Numerous roads and highways from Denver west into the foothills were closed including Interstate 70, US 40, US 285 and C-470.  Numerous vehicles became stuck in the heavy wet snowfall, with rates up to 2 inches per hour making the roadways impassable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  14 inches at Loveland Pass, 12 inches at Copper Mountain, 11.5 inches near Silverthorne, 11 inches at Keystone, and 10 inches at Breckenridge and Williams Fork Reservoir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weak shortwaves moved through in the days before this avalanche, depositing 4-8 inches of fresh snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Avalanche danger was rated as Moderate (2/5) on the day of the accident at the location. Accident involved a party of two backcountry skiers and a dog headed to an area known locally as Birthday Chute in the Pitkin Creek drainage, northeast of the town of Vail. They triggered the avalanche while climbing the slope, and were swept 2000 feet downhill. The first skier was partially buried but was able to self-extricate and suffered injury to their arm, the second skier was not buried but had injured both a leg and hand and was unable to move. The dog was partially buried but was freed by the first skier. The first skier was able to go for help, the second skier was airlifted to the hospital, and the dog was carried out by the rescue team.

A deep Atmospheric River and strong southerly flow moved into the Four Corners region, bringing widespread moderate to heavy snow to the southern mountains of southeast Utah and southwest Colorado, expanding into the central Colorado mountains. Snow continued in colder northwest flow behind the trough, bringing additional moderate to heavy snow to the northwest Colorado mountains and higher valleys. Milder temps limited accumulations in lower elevations with all mountain areas seeing significant snowfall. Highest wind gusts remained above 12,000 feet with gusts 45 to 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area. Wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were observed at elevations above 12,000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved from northwest to southeast across all of eastern Utah and western Colorado, with significant banding and a long lived snow squall that accompanied the cold front. This resulted in strong winds with gusts 45 to 55 mph in the valleys along the boundary with gusts 60 to 80 mph in the mountains.  Low visibilities and whiteout conditions also occurred at times along this frontal boundary due to falling and blowing snow.  Accumulations in the lower elevations were 2 inches or less in spots due to the brief nature of the snowfall along the front as well as milder temperatures, but the impacts due to the snow squalls were felt across much of the region as temperatures plummeted behind it. The San Juan mountains received the heaviest snow with the central Colorado mountains receiving moderate snowfall accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 15 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of 20 to 24 inches at Sharkstooth and Columbus Basin. Winds gusted in the 55 to 70 mph range for elevations above 11,000 feet with peak wind gust of 79 mph at Putney at elevations above 12,000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow developed on the evening of the 9th and continued through midday on 10th.  Nearly 14 inches over central Weld County, where snow fell at a rate of 1-2 inches per hour for several hours, while the Front Range Foothills, the rest of the urban corridor and plains, generally picked up 4 to 8 inches of snow.  The snow redeveloped on the evening on the 10th, melting snow combined with freezing temperatures, produced very icy and slick road conditions.  Multiple accidents occurred around the Denver metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting snow combined with freezing temperatures, produced very icy and slick road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy wet snow occurred in and near the Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide.  Storm totals ranged from 8 to nearly 20 inches in those areas, including the western and southern Denver suburbs.  Further north and east, storm totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches in Fort Collins/Loveland, as well as eastern areas of metro Denver.  Denver International Airport recorded 0.72 of precipitation on the 3rd, which tied this storm for the 3rd wettest for the month of February, going back to 1872.  Numerous roads and highways from Denver west into the foothills were closed including Interstate 70, US 40, US 285 and C-470.  Numerous vehicles became stuck in the heavy wet snowfall, with rates up to 2 inches per hour making the roadways impassable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  17 inches northwest of Horsetooth Mountain, 16.9 inches, 7 miles southeast of Cherokee Park, 16 inches near Buckhorn Mountain, 15.2 inches near Sunshine, 14.9 inches near Virginia Dale, and 12.8 inches near Mishawaka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the morning to mid afternoon on the 27th, strong winds moved over eastern Colorado  gusty over 50 mph. Meanwhile a boundary moved through the area and caused bands of snow to form. As these bands of snow traversed the region, the winds, still gusting up to 40 mph near the time of the snow, reduced visibility down to 1/4 mile. These blizzard-like conditions only lasted for a brief time after the snow began, as the winds weakened as a snow band moved over. However, travel was dangerous during the onset of the snow squall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall.  A co-op observer reported visibility of 1/4 mile in heavy snowfall. Also, a Viaero camera in Cheyenne Wells showed visibility of 1/4 mile or less in snow due to a slowly moving snow squall. Nearby mesonets recorded wind gusts ranging from 20 to 40 mph during the time the snow bands were moving through the county.||Key Impacts:  white-out conditions, snow packed roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rich monsoonal moisture and a passing wave combined with typical diurnal heating over the higher terrain to spawn scattered to widespread showers and thunderstorms over the northeastern Utah and northwestern Colorado. While the main threats with these storms were expected to be small hail, gusty winds, and lightning, in a few spots they also produced localized heavy rainfall. Slow storm motion kept these heavy raining storms over the same location for a prolonged period of time, triggering some debris flows over the steeper terrain of the Colorado headwaters region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple mudslides were reported by local law enforcement near Redstone, CO. These mudslides followed the Crystal River valley, along State Highway 133 near mile marker 53. This is roughly 1 mile north of Redstone. Slow storm motion, steep terrain, and heavy rain all combined in an area known to be susceptible to debris flows and mudslides. Highway 133 was closed for several hours for cleanup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Currant Creek Fire started in late July due to unknown causes 5 miles northwest of Cedaredge, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Currant Creek Fire started in late July due to unknown causes 5 miles northwest of Cedaredge, Colorado. The fire grew to 193 acres before it was contained in early August. The estimated final cost was $5 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Kings Canyon Fire started in late July due to a lightning strike a half mile south of the Little Dolores River near Kings Canyon about 15 miles southwest of Fruita, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kings Canyon Fire started in late July due to a lightning strike a half mile south of the Little Dolores River near Kings Canyon about 15 miles southwest of Fruita, Colorado. The fire grew to 220 acres before it was contained in early August. The estimated final cost was $1 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just enough moisture and instability lingered across the higher terrain of eastern Utah and western Colorado to form showers and thunderstorms. The most intense convection was observed across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado, where a passing weak shortwave and a modest upper level jet provided additional lift and instability. The probability of strong winds was also enhanced by dry surface levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the afternoon moved into evening, convection that had fired over the terrain in northeastern Utah gradually moved into northwest Colorado. Enhanced by a passing shortwave and a modest upper level jet, these showers and storms were capable of producing strong to severe winds, which were only enhanced by dry surface levels. Gusts associated with this event ranged from 45 mph to the peak of 60 mph, which was recorded at the KRIL ASOS at Garfield Regional Airport in Rifle. No reports of damage or injuries were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bucktail Fire started in early August 4 miles northeast of Nucla. The cause remains undetermined.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bucktail Fire started in early August 4 miles northeast of Nucla. The cause remains undetermined. The fire grew to 7,202 acres before it was contained in late August. The estimated final cost was $10 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence pattern developed early in the month of August, where just enough moisture and instability would linger between monsoonal surges to allow showers and thunderstorms to form over the higher terrain, and then drift over adjacent valleys. With a dry surface layer present, these storms would be capable of producing strong to severe wind gusts on occasion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm that developed on the higher terrain of the western edge of the Uncompahgre Plateau moved over the Colorado Department of Transportation's Mesonet site 070w001 Utah State Line. This mesonet site recorded a wind gust to 61 mph with this storm, likely due to dry surface layers enhancing outflow winds. No reports of damages or injuries were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With deep monsoonal moisture in place, as well as around 1000 J/kg of instability and some bulk shear aloft, widespread strong to severe thunderstorms formed across the Four Corners region. This resulted in multiple severe threats in eastern Utah, but was limited to heavier rain, non-severe hail, and some severe wind gusts in southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite the well saturated atmosphere, other parameters contributed to the development of a strong to severe thunderstorm over southern La Plata county. In addition to heavy rainfall, this storm produced a wind gust to 63 mph at the KDRO ASOS at Durango-La Plata County Airport. No reports of damage or injury were received in association with this gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rich monsoonal moisture and a passing wave combined with typical diurnal heating over the higher terrain to spawn scattered to widespread showers and thunderstorms over the northeastern Utah and northwestern Colorado. While the main threats with these storms were expected to be small hail, gusty winds, and lightning, in a few spots they also produced localized heavy rainfall. Slow storm motion kept these heavy raining storms over the same location for a prolonged period of time, triggering some debris flows over the steeper terrain of the Colorado headwaters region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of the same storms that led to a mudslide on Interstate 70. Mudslides were reported along Squaw Creek Drive in Cordillera, south of Edwards. Road and bridge crews were dispatched to clean up the debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cut off low pressure system that has been over the region the last few days finally was departing to the east, leaving behind a moisture laden air mass and areas of locally dense fog across the Central Yampa River Basin in northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized pockets of dense fog developed across the Central Yampa River Basin during the early morning hours of May 11th. This included near the Craig-Moffat County Airport, where the KCAG ASOS reported visibilities down to 1/4 mile or less at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under high pressure and with rich monsoonal moisture in place, showers and thunderstorms formed over the higher terrain of western Colorado on the afternoon of August 5th. Steering flow aloft was weak, leading to slow storm motion with any storm that formed, which combined with locally heavy rain to create a flash flood and debris flow threat in areas of steep terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a slow moving storm over the Vail Pass area of Interstate 70 resulted in a small mud or rock slide early in the afternoon of August 5th. This slide was spotted via Colorado Department of Transportation webcams, and did result in a brief closure of one lane of Interstate 70 for cleanup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies behind a band of early morning showers and thunderstorms associated with a departing shortwave disturbance led to localized areas of dense fog across portions of the Central Yampa River Basin in northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized pockets of dense fog developed across the Central Yampa River Basin during the early morning hours of May 15th. This included near the Craig-Moffat County Airport, where the KCAG ASOS reported visibilities down to 1/4 mile or less at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Spruce Creek Fire started in mid May from a lightning strike 15 miles northeast of Cortez.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Spruce Creek Fire started in mid May from a lightning strike 15 miles northeast of Cortez. The fire grew to 5,699 acres before it was contained in late May. The fire is estimated to have cost $3 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a weak cold front and low pressure trough that tracked across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado. Given the dry air at the lower levels, thunderstorms were high based with gusty outflow winds being the primary threat from storms. The stronger storms produced gusty winds in excess of 58 mph across Eagle County along Interstate 70. Eagle Street was closed for 40 minutes from 2nd St to Lost Lane in Gypsum due to a fallen tree likely due to the strong thunderstorm outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 64 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at Dowd Junction, 1 mile north of Dowd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an unseasonably strong Pacific trough moved inland in the days leading up to this wind event, the pressure gradient ahead of it began to tighten. An accompanying deep layer jet streak brought an additional boost to winds ahead of this trough. Although the winds began early on the morning of the 5th, they lasted through the cold front passage on the 7th, particularly at higher elevations. Multiple wind highlights, with a combination of High Wind Warnings and Wind Advisories, were in effect for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds across the region ranged from 45 mph to 60 mph during over the course of the multiday wind event, with the peak gust of 60 mph occurring on the afternoon of the 5th. This gust was recorded at the Dragon Road RAWS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an unseasonably strong Pacific trough moved inland in the days leading up to this wind event, the pressure gradient ahead of it began to tighten. An accompanying deep layer jet streak brought an additional boost to winds ahead of this trough. Although the winds began early on the morning of the 5th, they lasted through the cold front passage on the 7th, particularly at higher elevations. Multiple wind highlights, with a combination of High Wind Warnings and Wind Advisories, were in effect for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts ranged from 45 mph to 65 mph, with a peak gust of 66 mph occurring the KEEO ASOS in Meeker, CO on the afternoon of the 5th. Four of the 5 gusts over 58 mph occurred at the KEEO ASOS during this time frame. The other gust was 61 mph at the Pinto, Massadona 18SE RAWS, also on the afternoon of the 5th. The majority of peak gusts occurred on the 5th, although a few occurred with the cold frontal passage on the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an unseasonably strong Pacific trough moved inland in the days leading up to this wind event, the pressure gradient ahead of it began to tighten. An accompanying deep layer jet streak brought an additional boost to winds ahead of this trough. Although the winds began early on the morning of the 5th, they lasted through the cold front passage on the 7th, particularly at higher elevations. Multiple wind highlights, with a combination of High Wind Warnings and Wind Advisories, were in effect for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts ranged from 45 mph to 85 mph across the zone, with a peak recorded gust of 84 mph recorded at the Colorado Department of Transportation Mesonet site 139s034 Douglas Pass during the late morning on the 5th. This mesonet station recorded all four gusts over 75 mph, which all occurred on the 5th. Additional gusts under threshold but above 45 mph occurred with the cold front passage on the 7th, and a questionable wind gust to 141 mph at the Douglas Pass mesonet was recorded on the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant monsoonal moisture had advected into the region in the days prior to August 9th. By the 9th, the atmosphere had saturated enough to promote a widespread heavy rain threat with afternoon convection. In addition, upper level support in the form of divergence aloft in the right entrance region of an upper level jet also worked to support widespread strong convection. Storm motion was reasonably quick, but training of storms was anticipated, enhancing the threat of heavy rain, flash flooding, and debris flows. Areas of steep terrain in the central Colorado mountains were the most favored to see that flash flood and debris flow potential.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm caused a mudslide on Highway 64 at mile marker 57, approximately 17 miles west of Meeker.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm produced a minor mudslide on Highway 82 at mile marker 12, about 1 mile north of Carbondale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A distinct moisture gradient existed ahead of and behind a cold front situated across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado. Scattered showers and storms developed ahead of this boundary and given dry air at low levels, storms were high based producing virga and strong outflow winds. Wind gusts exceeded 58 mph in La Plata county due to virga.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 66 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Durango-La Plata County Regional Airport due to virga showers.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 68 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Eagle County Regional Airport. A tree was knocked down in Gypsum around the same time that these thunderstorm wind gusts occurred.

Peak gusts occurred mainly on the 5ht, although some gusts occurred with the cold frontal passage on the 7th. A total of 3 recorded gusts were at or above 58 mph.

The peak gust recorded at the KMTJ ASOS at the Montrose Regional Airport was 51 mph on the morning of the 7th. Additionally all the sites in this zone recorded gusts above 45 mph each day during this multiple day event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm produced a mudslide along Highway 50 between mile marker 181 and 182 which closed the highway in both directions. This occurred approximately 25 miles east of Gunnison or 7 miles south-southwest of Waunita Hot Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plenty of monsoonal moisture was present in western Colorado on August 13th, which did lead to a slow start of convection thanks to morning cloudiness. However, once skies cleared, ample instability and some additional lift from a passing jet streak helped to create widespread showers and thunderstorms over the higher terrain. With the abundance of deep, tropical moisture, training or slow moving storms weren't necessary to create flooding and debris flow concerns, and indeed on this day storms moved fairly quickly and did not train but debris flows still occurred. Ultimately, all it takes to create issues during such a setup is steep, susceptible terrain and one storm with high rainfall rates over that location.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Department of Transportation reported a closure along US Highway 50 roughly 10 miles east-southeast of Waunita Hot Springs, in Gunnison County. This area is on the climb up to Monarch Pass, meaning it is an area of very steep terrain that can be prone to rock and mudslides with heavy rainfall. The road had to be briefly closed for cleanup of the debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With above normal moisture in place across eastern Utah and western Colorado, and passing shortwaves adding some extra lift and instability, round after round of daily showers and thunderstorms had already been ongoing for 2-3 days prior to August 18th. This meant that, by the afternoon of the 18th, the atmospheric profile was fully saturated, meaning any stronger shower or storm could produce heavy rainfall and high rainfall rates. In addition, there was some indication that training of storms would be possible during the afternoon hours, heightening the risk for heavy rain, flash flooding, and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early in the afternoon of August 18th, Law Enforcement reported a mudslide on Colorado State Highway 145 at mile marker 83, or about 3 miles north of the town of Sawpit or 1.5 miles south of the town of Placerville. This is an area well known for mudslides and debris flows with heavy rainfall thanks to very steep terrain. Time of incident was based on radar data, as Law Enforcement report came in after the fact. Road was closed for a short while to facilitate cleanup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an unseasonably strong Pacific trough moved inland in the days leading up to this wind event, the pressure gradient ahead of it began to tighten. An accompanying deep layer jet streak brought an additional boost to winds ahead of this trough. Although the winds began early on the morning of the 5th, they lasted through the cold front passage on the 7th, particularly at higher elevations. Multiple wind highlights, with a combination of High Wind Warnings and Wind Advisories, were in effect for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to 95 mph, with the peak gust of 96 mph being recorded at KMYP AWOS on Monarch Pass on the morning of the 7th. Peak gusts occurred just prior to cold frontal passage on the 7th, but gusts above 45 mph began occurring on the morning of the 5th. The other gust recorded above 75 mph was recorded on the morning of the 6th, also at KMYP, indicating that high winds occurred for the duration of this event here.

This was the only gust above 58 mph, although numerous gusts into the 50s mph were recorded across the zone on all three days of the event. The peak gust recorded at KCEZ ASOS at the Cortez-Montezuma County Airport was 54 mph on the morning of the 5th.

The peak recorded gust of 58 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 6th at KDRO ASOS at the Durango-La Plata County Airport. Peak gusts occurred during the late night hours of the 5th into the early morning hours of the 6th, with a secondary peak on the afternoon of the 7th associated with the cold frontal passage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions for organized convection were less favorable on August 19th in comparison to previous days, with plenty of moisture but very limited upper level support or instability. However, the terrain of western Colorado acts as a source of lift, and differential daytime heating between the lower and higher valleys can add a boost of instability to that already present with the abundant monsoonal moisture. It was just this scenario on the afternoon of the 19th that allowed showers and storms to form over the San Juan mountains over Hinsdale county. The abundant moisture combined with slow storm motion to increase the threat of heavy rain, flash flooding, and debris flows across that area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Colorado Department of Transportation reported the closure of Colorado State Highway 149 at mile marker 74, roughly 1 mile north-northwest of Lake City, due to a mudslide. This area is very steep, and prone to rock and mudslides with either prolonged moderate to heavy rainfall, or short but intense heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August 20th was characterized by a relative downturn in available moisture as some drier air worked in from the northwest. With the lack of upper level support, the higher terrain would preferentially see shower and thunderstorm development. A weak moisture gradient set up across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado during the afternoon, providing an additional focus for instability and lift, and it was this gradient that eventually spawned a stronger storm capable of producing heavy rainfall and a debris flow in Rio Blanco County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Colorado Department of Transportation reported Colorado State Highway 64 closed at mile marker 56, between Wray Gulch Road and Indian Valley, due to a mudslide. This was just north of White River City, or 18 miles west of the town of Meeker along Highway 64. The road remained closed for several hours while cleanup was undertaken. This stretch of Highway 64 runs along the base of a steep cliff, leaving it prone to debris and mudflows during intense, heavy rainfall events.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another monsoonal surge of moisture began the night before August 21st, which combined with some lift associated with a jet aloft to create scattered to widespread shower and thunderstorm activity on the afternoon of the 21st. These showers and storms had a fast storm motion of 20-25 miles per hour, but some training was evident. This, combined with more than ample moisture, meant that the threat for heavy rain and debris flows was present across vulnerable locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Colorado Department of Transportation reported a mudslide and subsequent road closure of Colorado State Highway 82 at approximately mile marker 11, or between Satank Road turn off and Colorado State Highway 133. The road was closed for roughly 2 hours, reopening by 00:15AM MDT, or 23:15 MST. Colorado State Highway 82 in the vicinity of Carbondale is known to be susceptible to flash flooding and mud and debris flows will heavy, intense rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southwesterly jet aloft was in place of eastern Utah and western Colorado, not only advecting rich monsoonal moisture into the area, but also providing enhanced lift a some decent wind shear. All this to say the environment was perfect for stronger, and more organized convection to form during the afternoon hours of August 25th. With the above normal moisture and indications of potential training thunderstorms, as well as the fact of the unusually supportive environment itself, the local Grand Junction office issued a Flash Flood Watch for portions of the area, in addition to a moderate risk of excessive rainfall from the Weather Prediction Center and a slight risk of severe weather from the Storm Prediction Center, prompting a Severe Thunderstorm Watch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported water, rocks, mud, and other debris over Colorado State Highway 139 between mile markers 29 and 30, or about 5 miles south-southeast of Douglas Pass. The area around and south of Douglas Pass is very steep and prone to rock, mud, and debris flows under heavy, intense rainfall such as what fell on the afternoon of August 25th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall to the northeast of Mesa Verde National Park fell on the afternoon of August 25th, with estimated rainfall rates in excess of 2 inches per hour for around 10 minutes. That puts the rainfall total along County Road 41, in this area, at 0.25-0.35 inches. The debris flow resulted in the closure of the road and damage to the road in at least 2 locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager for Montezuma County reported a large boulder loosened from multiple consecutive days of heavy rain having fallen onto Road G, near the intersection of Road 21, about 2.5 miles from Cortez-Montezuma County Airport. This large boulder necessitated heavy machinery to clean up, but did not impact traffic on the road, despite partially blocking the west-bound lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As snow began to melt at an increased pace late in the month of May, river levels once again increased across portions of northwest Colorado. In particular, in Steamboat Springs once water levels rise above roughly 3.5 feet, portions of the Yampa River Core Trail begin to flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media reports began in late April of nuisance flooding along the Yampa Core Trail, and by the end of the month of May the Grand Junction Service hydrologist visited the area and was able to confirm the flooding was ongoing. River levels increased above the roughly 3.5 foot threshold for this trail flooding on the 14th, and remained above this through the end of the month. Peak river height of roughly 5.25 feet occurred on the 21st before gradually decreasing. A secondary, smaller peak of 4.75 feet occurred on the 29th, likely enhanced by precipitation in the area.

CZ_NAME: TRINIDAD VICINITY / LOWER HUERFANO RIVER BASIN & WESTERN LAS ANIMAS COUNTY BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively weak trough passed over southern Colorado from 1/25 to 1/26 producing moderate to heavy snowfall across the mountains, the mountain valleys, and portions of the I-25 Corridor. With the passing trough the vorticity advection sparked showers over the mountains the created snow bands over the mountains that spilled over the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 7 inches of snow that fell.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds develop during the morning hours of May 6th. The strong winds persisted for much of the day and decrease over the Denver area and lower foothills. Meanwhile, over the higher foothills and mountains, the strong winds persisted through the day and into the evening as well. An area across southern Denver and into northern Douglas Counties saw a blast of strong winds to 75 mph during the early morning hours of May 6th, which caused damage minor damage to 10 planes and major damage to 2 planes at the Centennial Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Centennial Airport (APA) a wind gust of 65 knots was recorded at 0528 MST. Local media also reported a small plane was flipped over by the winds. Airport spokesperson said heavy wind gusts caused damage to 12 parked aircraft. Sometime between 0515 and 0530 MST, the wind overturned one of those planes, a single-engine, fixed-wing AT-4. That plane and the plane it blew into both sustained serious damage. The other 10 aircraft had minor damage. ||Large overhead highway sign and pole toppled onto I-25. One semi truck was struck. No injuries reported. All lanes of southbound I-25 were blocked. Road crews were able to clear late in the morning.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wraparound moisture drove easterly flow into the upslope areas of the central Colorado mountains with banded precipitation forming on the northern side of a low pressure system as it was exiting to the east. This banded precipitation allowed for moderate to heavy snowfall rates to fall during the overnight hours across the southern portion of the Elk Mountains and northern portion of the West Elk Mountains and Sawatch Mountains.  Given the late season nature of this event, this snowfall was significant but impacts remained minimal as roads remained wet with snow accumulating mainly at elevations above 11,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 11 inches of snow fell across the southern half of the area at elevations above 11,000 feet. 6 to 8 inches of snow fell at the Snowmass and Aspen Ski Areas.
