A cold front stalled out just north of the Colorado/Wyoming border late on the 16th and into the early hours of the 17th. This front remained in place through the evening of the 18th before an approaching wave finally pushed it out of the way. Ahead (south) of this boundary, the pressure gradient tightened up enough to allow for some strong wind gusts across portions of northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to near 60 mph on the 17th and the 18th. The peak recorded wind gusts were both 58 mph, one recorded at the Great Divide 2SW RAWS on the evening of the 17th, and the other at the Pinto, Massadona 18SE RAWS on the evening of the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled out just north of the Colorado/Wyoming border late on the 16th and into the early hours of the 17th. This front remained in place through the evening of the 18th before an approaching wave finally pushed it out of the way. Ahead (south) of this boundary, the pressure gradient tightened up enough to allow for some strong wind gusts across portions of northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to 85 mph. The peak gust of 85 mph occurred at the Pine Ridge, Cameo 6NW RAWS late on the evening of the 18th. The wind gusts across this zone seemed to peak as the shortwave was pushing the stalled front out of its position near the Wyoming border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening trough over the Pacific Northwest phased with a potent shortwave over the Baja Peninsula, pulling a strong jet northeastward toward the Four Corners. This, combined with generally clear skies that promoted deep mixing, allowed strong winds to reach the surface on the afternoon of the 20th. This event was confined mainly to Southeast Utah and Southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds across the zone ranged from 45 mph to near 65 mph. The peak recorded gust of 66 mph was recorded at KDRO ASOS at the Durango-La Plata County Airport on the evening of the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As yet another strong Pacific trough approached eastern Utah and western Colorado with a cold front in tow, strong and gusty southwest winds broke out across the region. The recorded gusts were clustered in time, all occurring within about an hour and a half of each other, likely reflecting the period of passage of the cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout tornado occurred over southern Washington county. The other severe weather accompanied a supercell thunderstorm that tracked southeast across eastern Logan County which produced 2 tornadoes and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down just west of CR 59, along CR 56, moving towards the east-southeast. The tornado impacted one residence after initial touchdown uprooting and snapping several trees on the west side of the property. A few pieces of hard blue plastic window well covering was found embedded into the west facing stucco-ed wall of the home. The tornado then proceeded to impact another residence about one-quarter mile east-southeast where it removed the west facing awning, anchored to the primary houses' roof structure, the northwest facing cement board house siding, and approximately one square of asphalt roofing shingles. The most significant impact was to three telephone poles on CR 59, about one-tenth of a mile south of the CR 56, that were snapped at their bases, resulting in EF-2 damage. The tornado remained on the ground for a few more minutes moving primarily towards the east where it overturned a few center pivot and damaging a working farm's outbuilding. The tornado then lifted over open fields. It was reported, by a trained spotter, that the tornado briefly touched down once more just east of the Caliche School before dissipating. No additional damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system entered eastern Colorado on the 6th and quickly moved east. There was a steep pressure rise as the low was leaving the area through the 7th. These sharp changes in pressure caused winds to gust up to 69 MPH near Yuma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple mesonet sites across the county reported winds gusting over 58 MPH for over 17 hours, including a personal weather station in Yuma that recorded an 80 MPH gust. Dispatch also reported trees had been blown down in Yuma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave trough drove a strong cold front through eastern Utah and western Colorado during the day prior to the freeze event, with unseasonably cold air filtering in behind the front. This in combination with light winds and clear skies allowed temperatures to fall several degrees below normal for early May, including below freezing in many agricultural parts of the region where the growing season was already well underway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With an unusually cold airmass in place, and calm winds and clear skies promoting effective radiational cooling, temperatures dropped below freezing across much of the zone. 7 of the 10 sites available recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, with the average duration of these temperatures being 5 hours. Only 3 sites recorded temperatures at or below 28F for at least 3 hours, with an average duration being 2.5 hours. The lowest temperature recorded was 22F at a Colorado State Agricultural Mesonet station. The KRIL ASOS at the Garfield Regional Airport in Rifle, CO recorded a minimum temperature of 28F. Minimum temperatures ranged from 22F to 32F, with an average minimum of 29F. This event qualifies as a general Freeze, but not a Hard Freeze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system exited the High Plains, high winds developed on the back side of the low with winds out of the northwest. Sporadic gusts up to 69 mph were reported across Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties in East-central Colorado, with the peak gusts occurring near Arapahoe in Cheyenne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a duration of just over two hours, sporadic high wind gusts of 58 to 69 mph occurred across Cheyenne County. The 69 mph gust was recorded via a mesonet station near the town of Arapahoe. Key Impacts: Hazardous travel, high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds occurred ahead of a strong cold front that brought much colder air as well as moderate to heavy snow across the northern and central mountains of northeast Utah and northwest Colorado. The Elkhead and Park Range Mountains as well as the Flattop Mountains in northwest Colorado saw the heaviest snowfall amounts with snowfall accumulations over a foot and locally higher up to 2 feet. Moderate snow also occurred across the mountains of southeast Utah with lesser amounts across the San Juan mountains. Strong winds continued to accompany the snowfall due to the change in air mass resulting in blowing snow and low visibility at times, but road impacts were limited to the higher passes due to wet roads in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 16 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 27 inches at Tower. Winds gusted in the 60 to 65 mph range at Storm Peak Lab with a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic pattern within a longwave trough allowed for a series of major shortwave troughs to propagate over Colorado. This allowed for several days of severe thunderstorms.  Severe thunderstorms were fueled by the conditionally unstable environment with sufficient forcing, high amounts of mid-level moisture, high levels of buoyancy (CAPE), steep lapse rates, and effective bulk shear. This resulted in reports of large hail (in excess of 2 inches), severe wind gusts of greater than 50 kts, and even a few land spouts/funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple photos of a brief funnel cloud were reported on social media from different chasers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dynamic pattern within a longwave trough allowed for a series of major shortwave troughs to propagate over Colorado. This allowed for several days of severe thunderstorms.  Severe thunderstorms were fueled by the conditionally unstable environment with sufficient forcing, high amounts of mid-level moisture, high levels of buoyancy (CAPE), steep lapse rates, and effective bulk shear. This resulted in reports of large hail (in excess of 2 inches), severe wind gusts of greater than 50 kts, and even a few land spouts/funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter size hail, with a few bigger stones estimated at 1.5 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During late March, yet another deep upper level trough dug in across the western US. This, along with a strong southwesterly flow from the subtropical jet, brought in sufficient moisture and synoptic/orographic lift for another round of snowfall for parts of SE Colorado. Additionally, the strong synoptic forcing and convective nature of parts of this system did allow for the formation of one landspout tornado on March 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 12.9 inches of snow measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure began to retrograde to the west towards Arizona with drier air moving in from the north, keeping the subtropical moisture in place for areas south of Interstate 70. Enough moisture remained in addition to a shortwave moving through the flow to allow for a few strong storms still capable of moderate to heavy rainfall rates, that produced some localized flash flooding and mudslides in areas of steep terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall rates from a strong thunderstorm caused a mudslide to occur near Sawpit along Highway 145 between mile markers 76 and 80. This mudslide resulted in a closure of Highway 145 in both directions.

During late March, yet another deep upper level trough dug in across the western US. This, along with a strong southwesterly flow from the subtropical jet, brought in sufficient moisture and synoptic/orographic lift for another round of snowfall for parts of SE Colorado. Additionally, the strong synoptic forcing and convective nature of parts of this system did allow for the formation of one landspout tornado on March 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video from a local on social media revealed a landspout tornado feature near Pueblo West. The video showed clear circulation from cloud to ground, the landspout was on the ground for approximately 2 to 3 minutes, moving in an eastward direction. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure began to retrograde to the west towards Arizona with drier air moving in from the north, keeping the subtropical moisture in place for areas south of Interstate 70. Enough moisture remained in addition to a shortwave moving through the flow to allow for a few strong storms still capable of moderate to heavy rainfall rates, that produced some localized flash flooding and mudslides in areas of steep terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall rates of 0.5 to 1 inch in less than an hour from a strong thunderstorm resulted in flash flooding in Hells Canyon through Dinosaur National Monument. An additional inch accumulation of pea sized hail also accompanied the heavy rain resulting in the flash flooding.

This, along with a strong southwesterly flow from the subtropical jet, brought in sufficient moisture and synoptic/orographic lift for another round of snowfall for parts of SE Colorado. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mostly zonal flow aloft advected some modest midlevel moisture into northern portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado in the days leading up to this event. This moisture, combined with some low-end instability, set the stage form some isolated showers and thunderstorms to form over the higher terrain. A passing weak shortwave trough added some extra lift and energy to the region on the 1st, resulting in some stronger showers and thunderstorms capable of producing strong, gusty winds. In addition, dry surface levels led to precipitation from these storms evaporating before reaching the surface, thus also enhancing winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust associated with this thunderstorm was 58 mph and was recorded at the KCAG ASOS at the Craig-Moffat County Airport at 3:31 PM MDT. Additional gusts with this storm, while strong, were generally below severe criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering moisture after a monsoonal/post-tropical moisture surge combined with some enhanced instability and lift from a passing shortwave to produce scattered to widespread convection across eastern Utah and western Colorado. Drying surface levels allowed for some enhancement of outflow winds from showers and storms as falling precipitation evaporated before reaching the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moving over the city of Montrose late in the afternoon of the 24th produced strong to severe wind gusts, including one gust to 70 mph. This gust was recorded at the KMTJ ASOS at Montrose Regional Airport at 6:50 PM MDT. Damage was associated with this storm, with reports of several large tree limbs blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With lingering midlevel moisture, modest instability, and a weak wave moving through the flow aloft, stronger showers and thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain north of I-70. Dry surface levels helped to enhance thunderstorm outflow winds, with peak gusts generally below severe criteria, but at least one gust above criteria recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pinto RAWS station recorded a gust to 60 mph with a passing thunderstorm. This storm also produced a gust to 49 mph at the KEEO ASOS at Meeker Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure tracking east over California nudged the subtropical high eastward into the Southern Plains in the days leading up to this event. This allowed some monsoonal moisture to trickle into the Four Corners region. On the day of the event, the low crossed the Four Corners, adding some extra lift and instability. The most widespread coverage was south of the I-70 corridor, although that didn't stop a few showers and storms from firing off the higher terrain along and north of I-70. Dry surface levels kept the main threats to being gusty winds and maybe some small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With the increased monsoonal moisture at midlevels, scattered showers and thunderstorms built on the higher terrain near Cortez. Dry surface levels acted to enhance outflow winds as storms moved overhead. These storms eventually moved over Cortez, they produced gusts to 58 mph at the KCEZ ASOS at Cortez-Montezuma Airport in Cortez. Gusts to 58 mph were recorded twice, once at 1:02 PM MDT and again at 1:09 PM MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave in the flow around the dominant high pressure moved across eastern Utah and western Colorado on the 19th. This wave provided some enhanced instability and lift to the already modest instability and lift, as well as residual moisture, leading to a round of widespread showers and thunderstorms. However, with modest parameters and very dry surface layers, evidenced by inverted-V soundings, storms that did form were most favored to produce virga and strong, gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A round of showers and thunderstorms tracked across central Rio Blanco county, producing wind gusts ranging from 45 to 60 mph. The peak recorded gust was 58 mph, and this was recorded at KEEO ASOS, located at Meeker Airport. No reports of damages or injuries were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the middle of a monsoonal surge, plenty of deep moisture was available to fuel showers and thunderstorms on the afternoon of the 27th. In addition, modest instability and enhanced lift and shear from an upper level jet were also in place, leading to an increased chance of stronger storms forming. The Storm Prediction Center place the region under a marginal risk for severe weather, highlight severe wind and hail threats. The hail did not materialize, but the winds did.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms tracked across Rio Blanco and Routt counties on the afternoon of the 27th, producing strong to severe wind gusts as they did so. Recorded wind gusts ranged from 50 mph to 60  mph, with the peak gust being recorded at the PIRC2 Pinto mesonet site, 18 miles southeast of Massadona. Gusts into the 50s were recorded at KEEO ASOS at Meeker Airport and KHDN AWOS at Yampa Valley Airport in Hayden. No reports of damages or injuries were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon hours, a line of thunderstorms developed over East-Central Colorado and moved southeast into Kit Carson County. The tail end storm of the line produced a swath of accumulating hail up to golf ball in size from the northwest side of Seibert to southwest of Stratton. The largest hail fell in Seibert and southwest of Stratton. There was some hail damage to a vehicle traveling on I-70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Alexander Mountain Fire started on Monday, July 29, 2024, north of US Highway 34 and west of Loveland, Colorado. Evacuations and road closures were in effect for several days. A total of 28 homes and 21 outbuildings lost in the fire. Final acreage of the fire has been mapped at 9,668 acres.||US Highway 34 between Loveland and Estes Park fully reopened on August 8, 2024. All evacuation orders were lifted as of 10:48 a.m. on August 12, exactly two weeks from the time the Alexander Mountain Fire was first reported to dispatch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep subtropical moisture plume remained in place as a deep low pressure trough and cold front moved across the area, resulting in strong thunderstorms that produced heavy rain rates and flash flooding in some areas. One particular storm resulted in a debris flow across Pitkin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain rates from a thunderstorm resulted in mudslides across Highway 133 about 4 miles north of Redstone, resulting in a closure of the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep subtropical moisture remained trapped underneath an area of high pressure, resulting in slow moving thunderstorms with moderate to heavy rainfall rates and small hail. One particular storm resulted in multiple debris flows over the Sawpit area in San Miguel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from slow moving thunderstorms resulted in multiple mudslides along Highway 145 between mile markers 72 and 77, south of Sawpit. There were reports of 4 mudslides between mile markers 72 and 77 on Highway 145 between 1545 and 1600 MST. One, possibly two, bigger slides occurred between mile markers 76 and 77 on Highway 145. This resulted in a closure of Highway 145 between mile markers 72 and 78 in both directions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed in the afternoon hours of July 20th across the northeastern plains of Colorado, producing a few severe wind gusts. A more prolific storm developed near the Wyoming border and tracked south across the I-25 corridor through the evening hours, producing a swath of severe hail and wind from Fort Collins into the Denver metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media source provided photos of flattened and shredded crops from a combination of wind and hail at a malthouse east of Campion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed in the afternoon hours of July 20th across the northeastern plains of Colorado, producing a few severe wind gusts. A more prolific storm developed near the Wyoming border and tracked south across the I-25 corridor through the evening hours, producing a swath of severe hail and wind from Fort Collins into the Denver metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media source provided numerous photos of crop damage due to hail and wind at a malthouse. Significant crop loss was noted across 120 acres of distillers corn, 12 acres of trial blue corn, 300 acres of alfalfa and 70 acres of Genie barley. All of the corn and barley acreage was considered a total loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With high pressure remaining to our west, drier air was pushing in across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado with deep subtropical moisture remaining pooled across southeast Utah and southwest Colorado. This allowed for a few stronger storms with localized heavy rainfall rates. One particular storm resulted in debris flows across La Plata County to the north of Durango.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall rates from a strong thunderstorm resulted in mudslides along Highway 550 to the north of Durango, reducing traffic to one lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure to the west continued to advect much drier air across northern and central portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado resulting in a downturn in storm coverage. However, a small pocket of deep subtropical moisture still remained over the eastern San Juans and Archuleta County, resulting in a few stronger storms that produced heavy rainfall rates and localized flash flooding. One particular storm resulted in a debris flow over Archuleta County near Chimney Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from strong thunderstorms resulted in a rock slide across Highway 151 in an area between the intersection of Forest Service Road 618 and Highway 160. Colorado Highway 151 was closed in both directions due to the rock slide between mile markers 32 and 33.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoonal moisture pushed in from the west during the overnight and early morning hours accompanied by a shortwave disturbance, allowing for stronger storms to form capable of heavy rainfall rates. This moisture replaced a previously dry air mass so heavy rain threat was more localized. One particular storm resulted in debris flows over Pitkin County that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced heavy rainfall rates resulting in a large mudslide at Avalanche Ranch along Highway 133.  There were other mudslides along Highway 133 between Redstone Blvd and Red Dog Rd about 4 miles north of Redstone. These mudslides resulted in Highway 133 being closed in both directions from mile markers 53 to 57.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A grass fire was sparked a few miles south of Byers late on the morning of July 13th, which quickly spread across dry vegetation. The fire was contained by 1600MST, but destroyed 1 house, 7 outbuildings, and burned a total of 1100 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to broadcast media, a small bird contacted an electrical line, igniting its feathers, which subsequently started a grass fire on the 2400 Block of Quail Hollow Road, southeast of Byers. North to northeast winds pushed the fire south through very dry vegetation, where the fire grew to a total of 1,155 acres in rural Arapahoe county. Law Enforcement and Broadcast Media reported a total of 1 home and 7 outbuildings damaged or destroyed from the fire, which was contained shortly after 1400MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure tracking east over California nudged the subtropical high eastward into the Southern Plains in the days leading up to this event. This allowed some monsoonal moisture to trickle into the Four Corners region. On the day of the event, the low crossed the Four Corners, adding some extra lift and instability. The most widespread coverage was south of the I-70 corridor, although that didn't stop a few showers and storms from firing off the higher terrain along and north of I-70. Dry surface levels kept the main threats to being gusty winds and maybe some small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Late in the day, a round of showers and thunderstorms moved east along the higher terrain of the central and upper Colorado River Valleys. These storms produced gusts above 45 mph, including one recorded gust to 58 mph at the Colorado Department of Transportation mesonet station 0.1 mile West of No Name Tunnel. An additional mesonet station approximately 12 miles to the east recorded a gust to 53 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Enough moisture remained in the atmosphere to fuel a few showers forming on the southern San Juan mountains. These showers and storms eventually drifted southward over southern La Plata county. With very dry surface air in place on this day, these showers and storms were predominantly producing gusty outflow winds and frequent lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the 11th, one of the storms drifting south off the San Juan mountains produced a wind gust to 60 mph over the Durango-La Plata County Airport. This gust was recorded by the KDRO ASOS at the airport. No other gusts of this magnitude were recorded, and no reports of damage or injury were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture associated with a weak monsoonal push led to the development of widespread showers and thunderstorms on the afternoon of the 14th. As this was the first day of the monsoonal surge, surface levels remained dry. This led to predominantly virga showers and storms, as well as strong, gusty outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed off the higher terrain in the western half of Moffat County, and gradually drifted east. As it did so, these storms produced a wind gust to 71 mph, that was recorded by the Mesonet site GDRC2, 2 miles southwest of Great Divide. This was the only recorded gust of this strength. No reports of injuries or damage were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep subtropical moisture increased across eastern Utah and western Colorado and combined with forcing from a strong low pressure trough digging across the Intermountain West to result in widespread severe thunderstorms. These storms produced severe wind gusts, large hail and heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved through Gateway with heavy rain rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour, where 1.18 inches of rain fell in a 30 minute period.  Flooding occurred due to the heavy rain rates where water moved wood and rocks into a nearby creek.  Internet service was lost during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced hail and heavy rainfall as they slowly drift south across the east/southeast part of the Denver Metro area. Additional storms track across Colorado producing hail up to 1 inch in Fairplay and Platteville. Plowable hail occurred under the Fairplay storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the one inch hail. CDOT web cameras showed hail covering 285 in and near Fairplay. Plowable hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually high moisture and instability values across eastern Utah and western Colorado led to widespread shower and thunderstorm activity on the 30th. These storms produced heavy rain and strong, gusty winds along with frequent lightning and some hail. The San Juan mountains and points south saw the most active weather of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm that formed near the Durango-La Plata County Airport produced heavy rainfall and strong, gusty winds as it moved through. Most recorded gusts were below severe thresholds, but one gust to 64 mph was recorded at the KDRO ASOS at 6:33 PM MDT. No reports of damages, injuries, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep subtropical moisture increased across eastern Utah and western Colorado and combined with forcing from a strong low pressure trough digging across the Intermountain West to result in widespread severe thunderstorms. These storms produced severe wind gusts, large hail and heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong to severe thunderstorm moved through the far western portion of Mesa County in Colorado. A trained spotter measured a gust to 65 mph at her home, 5 miles northwest of Mack. In addition, an automated Colorado Department of Transportation mesonet site recorded a gust to 57 mph near the Utah state line on I-70. This is roughly 10 miles southwest of the spotter's location. No reports of wind damage were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep subtropical moisture increased across eastern Utah and western Colorado and combined with forcing from a strong low pressure trough digging across the Intermountain West to result in widespread severe thunderstorms. These storms produced severe wind gusts, large hail and heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observer reported hail of an estimated 0.88 inches, comparable to a Nickle, in the Orchard Mesa area southeast of Grand Junction on the afternoon of the 27th. This hail was reported to have caused damaged to crops within a roughly 2 mile radius.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep subtropical moisture increased across eastern Utah and western Colorado and combined with forcing from a strong low pressure trough digging across the Intermountain West to result in widespread severe thunderstorms. These storms produced severe wind gusts, large hail and heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved over the city of Montrose, producing multiple severe threats including winds. The peak recorded gusts were both 62 mph, and both were recorded at the KMTJ ASOS at Montrose Regional Airport. These gusts were measured at 5:20 PM MDT and 5:40 PM MDT. Gusts with this storm ranged from 45 mph to the peak of 62 mph. No reports of damage were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant moisture remained to fuel some stronger storms across the region, but flooding was more localized in nature. One particular storm caused a mudslide across Highway 64 at mile marker 65 over Rio Blanco County, 11 miles west-southwest of Meeker.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a mudslide across Highway 64 at mile marker 65 over Rio Blanco County, 11 miles west-southwest of Meeker.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rattlesnake Gulch Fire started in mid April due to unknown causes 8 miles west of Delta in the Dominguez-Escalante National Conservation Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rattlesnake Gulch Fire started in mid April due to unknown causes 8 miles west of Delta in the Dominguez-Escalante National Conservation Area. The fire grew to 110 acres before it was contained in mid April. Seven power poles destroyed sending their lines into the Gunnison River, several hundred railroad ties damaged, and the historic Gunnison River waterwheel was heavily damaged with the wooden supports and flume destroyed. However, the wheel remains in tact.

These storms varied in their speed and strength, but overall eastern Colorado experienced baseball sized hail, flash flooding, a tornado, and straight line wind damage with observed speeds of 97 MPH and estimated speeds over 110 MPH.  No damage was observed from the tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS in Burlington started measuring severe wind gusts at 43 minutes after the hour with a 60 MPH gust. Over the next 5 minutes, the speeds increased to 96 MPH before the ASOS stopped reporting wind speed because the sensor was damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey crew recorded an area of 60-112 mph straight line winds. Peak wind in the area was 112 mph with damage to grain bins south of the Burlington Airport. Strongest measured gust was 97 mph at the Burlington ASOS. Other notable damage includes numerous trees with broken branches or trunks snapped, and an overturned pivot. The surveyed swath is triangular shaped with a 4 mile long north/south damaged area along Highway 385 centered around the Burlington airport. The swath continues 10 miles southeast from the airport, ending about 0.5 mile south of the junction of county roads J and 55.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours as a low pressure system moved over the High Plains. This cluster of storms produced up to penny sized hail near the town of Vona, Colorado then continued east-southeast to produced estimated 80 mph wind gusts south of Burlington that blew over an irrigation pivot and additional gusts of 55 to 58 mph near the town of Arapahoe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to penny sized hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low moved across Colorado April 15th on shift northeast of the state on the 16th. Mountain snow develop of the afternoon of 15th and was heavy at times Monday night especially over the northern mountains where SNOTEL sites 1-2 inches of precipitation. The heaviest amounts were around Cameron Pass where 2-2.25 inches of precipitation fell, likely leading to 2 feet of snowfall. The higher measured snowfall amounts were 20 inches at Copper Mountain ski area and 18 inches at  7.7 N of Granby, and 17 inches at Loveland Ski area.||Colorado Highway 14 west of Fort Collins was closed in both directions due to an avalanche on the morning of the 16th. The avalanche was between mile markers 61 and 78. ||Strong winds develop on the plains on the early on the 16th and continued into the afternoon hours. The strongest winds were found across northern Colorado where Julesburg gusted to 66 mph and Buckeye, just off of I-25 at Buckeye RAWS gusted to 62 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Here's a list of high wind reports: 62 mph wind gust at Buckeye RAWS and 60 mph I-25 and Buckeye Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across northeast Colorado on April 6th and then moved northeast into central Nebraska on April 7th. Strong and damaging winds occurred over portions of the mountains, foothills and northeast plains, from April 6th through April 7th, as the system moved across. Wind gusts across the mountains and along and near the foothills ranged from 70 to 95 mph with a peak gust of 97 mph at the NCAR Mesa Lab. Across the northeast plains, the strongest winds were focused along and north of a line from Denver to Fort Morgan to Akron. Winds gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common.||There were several reports of downed trees, power poles and other minor damage where the highest winds occurred. Up to 150,000 customers lost power during the peak of the event. Most of these outages were in and around the Denver area. More than 600 miles of lines were proactively de-energized by XCEL energy. (This is first time XCEL has done this).||Colorado State Patrol was notified of 10 toppled semi trucks throughout Colorado during the wind storm, most were on the Interstate 25 corridor and on Interstate 70. Numerous small fires were start due to blown over powerlines, including one house fire in Denver where powerlines fell on a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Here's a list of the high wind reports: US 385 Holyoke 72 MPH at 1817 PM 04/06, 8 S Holyoke 63 MPH, Holyoke Airport 62 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large Pacific low pressure system moved through the area and tapped into deeper moisture from the Gulf of Mexico as it moved onto the Plains, allowing this moisture to wraparound the low and bring moderate to heavy snowfall across the Northwest Colorado Mountains. Roads were impacted during the overnight period resulting in a closure over Vail Pass along Interstate 70. Gusty winds at the higher elevations generated areas of blowing snow. The lower elevations remained milder where rain fell and snow accumulations were limited.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 11 inches of snow fell across the area, with locally higher amounts up to 18 inches at Tower and Rabbit Ears Pass. Winds gusted 44 to 45 mph at Storm Peak Lab.

SNOTEL estimates reached 23 inches 3 north-northeast of Mount Audubon in Boulder County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with a low pressure trough moved through eastern Utah and western Colorado, bringing cold enough air to generate moderate snowfall at the higher elevations of the western Colorado mountains. Strong winds also generated areas of blowing snow among the higher elevations. Snowfall was very limited in the lower elevations due to the milder air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted to 55 mph at Storm Peak Lab.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure approached eastern Utah and western Colorado from the west, with a strong 140 knot jet aloft wrapping around the trough's base. This jet, combined with a very tight southwesterly pressure gradient aloft, brought very strong winds to the region ahead of an impressive cold front. Wind gusts ranged from 45 mph to 65 mph across valley locations, while mountain locations saw gusts up to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to near 60 mph during this event, with a peak recorded gust of 59 mph occurring at the Colorado Department of Transportation weather station in Dove Creek, CO. This was the only location to record a gust above 59 mph. The KCEZ ASOS recorded a peak gust of 54 mph.

A deep trough of low pressure approached eastern Utah and western Colorado from the west, with a strong 140 knot jet aloft wrapping around the trough's base. This jet, combined with a very tight southwesterly pressure gradient aloft, brought very strong winds to the region ahead of an impressive cold front. Wind gusts ranged from 45 mph to 65 mph across valley locations, while mountain locations saw gusts up to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to near 65 mph, with the peak recorded gust occurring at the Colorado Department of Transportation weather station located in Palisade, CO. This was the only location to record gusts above 58 mph. The KGJT ASOS at the Grand Junction Regional Airport recorded a peak gust of 55 mph.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As southwesterly wind strengthened across the Four Corners Region ahead of a potent cold front and unseasonably strong Pacific trough, these winds picked up dust from the deserts of northern Arizona. This dust, along with dust pick up across the lower elevations of southeast Utah, were transported as far north as the I-70 corridor in far western Colorado and eastern Utah, including the Grand Valley and areas around Moab.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of Blowing Dust across portions of Montezuma County, in Colorado Zone 021, with localized areas of visibility reduced to less that 1 mile. This blowing dust seemed to be concentrated around Sleeping Ute Mountain, and was focused at the surface level, with higher elevation vantage points providing improved visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As southwesterly wind strengthened across the Four Corners Region ahead of a potent cold front and unseasonably strong Pacific trough, these winds picked up dust from the deserts of northern Arizona. This dust, along with dust pick up across the lower elevations of southeast Utah, were transported as far north as the I-70 corridor in far western Colorado and eastern Utah, including the Grand Valley and areas around Moab.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds across the zone ranged from 40 to 55 mph, with peak gust of 64 mph occurring in Palisade. This wind helped carry blowing dust that significantly reduced visibility across the zone, with much of the Grand Valley and the northern Gunnison valley being unable to see local landmarks such as Colorado National Monument, the Grand Mesa, and the Bookcliffs. Combinations of webcams, social media posts, and NWS employee reports indicate visibility was reduced to less that 1 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system moving through the area brought cooler temperatures and increased moisture, which became trapped underneath valley inversions. This led to a short period of very localized dense fog to develop in the Grand Valley along the Colorado River. This did impact portions of I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized pockets of dense fog developed across the Grand Valley during the early morning hours of April 28th. This included near the Grand Junction Regional Airport, where the KGJT ASOS reported visibilities less than 1/2 mile. In addition, localized pockets of dense fog formed along the Colorado River between Grand Junction and the town of Fruita to the west, impacting a portion of I-70 that runs along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass had moved in over eastern Utah and western Colorado by the night of April 6th to the 7th. This already cold air was compounded by clear and calm conditions that promoted radiational cooling across much of the region. At this point in the year, only the lowest elevations of eastern Utah and western Colorado had acquired enough Growing Degree Days to warrant freeze highlights, as important agricultural assets, with particular emphasis on fruit trees, were beginning to bloom and a hard freeze event could be disastrous. Luckily, many of these crops were not damaged by this freeze event, even though many locations did hit hard freeze criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures across the zone ranged from 32F to 23F during this event. The lowest recorded temperature of 23F occurred at two sites, AV979 and E8921, both mesonet sites. The KGJT ASOS at the Grand Junction regional airport recorded a minimum temperature of 27F. 15 of the 18 available sites recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, with an average duration of 6 hours. Only 6 of the 18 sites recorded temperatures at or below 28 degrees for at least 3 hours, with an average duration of 3.5 hours. Based on these data, this event for this zone qualifies only as a Freeze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As snowmelt began to pick up at the end of the month of April, increased flows on the Yampa river through the town of Steamboat Springs led to multiple and various closures of the Yampa River Core Trail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from the City of Steamboat Springs on facebook informed followers that the Yampa River Core Trail would be closed periodically due to snowmelt flooding as river levels fluctuated. River levels during this period ranged from 3.5 ft to 4.5 ft, before dropping below 3 ft again.

CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across northeast Colorado on April 6th and then moved northeast into central Nebraska on April 7th. Strong and damaging winds occurred over portions of the mountains, foothills and northeast plains, from April 6th through April 7th, as the system moved across. Wind gusts across the mountains and along and near the foothills ranged from 70 to 95 mph with a peak gust of 97 mph at the NCAR Mesa Lab. Across the northeast plains, the strongest winds were focused along and north of a line from Denver to Fort Morgan to Akron. Winds gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common.||There were several reports of downed trees, power poles and other minor damage where the highest winds occurred. Up to 150,000 customers lost power during the peak of the event. Most of these outages were in and around the Denver area. More than 600 miles of lines were proactively de-energized by XCEL energy. (This is first time XCEL has done this).||Colorado State Patrol was notified of 10 toppled semi trucks throughout Colorado during the wind storm, most were on the Interstate 25 corridor and on Interstate 70. Numerous small fires were start due to blown over powerlines, including one house fire in Denver where powerlines fell on a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme winds occurred from late afternoon 4-6 to early morning 4-7 with numerous wind gusts in the 70-90 mph range and a few topping 90 mph over northern sections of zone 38. Here's a list of the higher end reports. 93 & 91 mph Buckeye RAWS, 84 & 82 mph 6 SW Carr, 79 mph at Natural Fort (CO/WY Border) and 5 NNW Nunn. Also in the Fort Collins area, the top reported gust reached 71 mph at Timnath. Some large trees were also blown over in the Fort Collins area. , Colorado State Patrol needed to close Interstate 25 from Wellington all the way north to the Wyoming state line on 4-6. Strong winds destroyed a new barn and rehab center at Chris P's Rescue and Rehabilitation (for pigs) in Pierce, CO.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low moved across Colorado April 15th on shift northeast of the state on the 16th. Mountain snow develop of the afternoon of 15th and was heavy at times Monday night especially over the northern mountains where SNOTEL sites 1-2 inches of precipitation. The heaviest amounts were around Cameron Pass where 2-2.25 inches of precipitation fell, likely leading to 2 feet of snowfall. The higher measured snowfall amounts were 20 inches at Copper Mountain ski area and 18 inches at  7.7 N of Granby, and 17 inches at Loveland Ski area.||Colorado Highway 14 west of Fort Collins was closed in both directions due to an avalanche on the morning of the 16th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong southwest winds occurred out ahead of a strong cold front and low pressure trough that moved through eastern Utah and western Colorado, bringing colder air with moderate snowfall across the mountains of western Colorado. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across northeast Colorado on April 6th and then moved northeast into central Nebraska on April 7th. Strong and damaging winds occurred over portions of the mountains, foothills and northeast plains, from April 6th through April 7th, as the system moved across. Wind gusts across the mountains and along and near the foothills ranged from 70 to 95 mph with a peak gust of 97 mph at the NCAR Mesa Lab. Across the northeast plains, the strongest winds were focused along and north of a line from Denver to Fort Morgan to Akron. Winds gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common.||There were several reports of downed trees, power poles and other minor damage where the highest winds occurred. Up to 150,000 customers lost power during the peak of the event. Most of these outages were in and around the Denver area. More than 600 miles of lines were proactively de-energized by XCEL energy. (This is first time XCEL has done this).||Colorado State Patrol was notified of 10 toppled semi trucks throughout Colorado during the wind storm, most were on the Interstate 25 corridor and on Interstate 70. Numerous small fires were start due to blown over powerlines, including one house fire in Denver where powerlines fell on a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strongest wind gust reached 91 mph at Breckenridge Ski resort on Peak 6 at 1300 MST. Additional high reports of 84 mph at Dakota Hill, 76 miles per hour Jones Hill RAWS, 72 mph Arapahoe Basin Ski area, 73 mph Loveland Pass, 72, mph Pickle Gulch RAWS. Power knocked out at Eldora ski area until late Monday (4-8). Eldora shut down operations 12:00 MST Saturday (4-6) and didn't resume until 8:00 MST Tuesday (4-9).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very deep tropical moisture associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Alberto combined with monsoonal moisture was forced through eastern Utah and western Colorado due to a large high pressure ridge over the Central U.S. and a deep low pressure trough over the Great Basin. This deep moisture combined with strong instability and shear to produce widespread severe weather across eastern Utah and western Colorado with thunderstorms producing severe wind gusts, large hail and heavy rain which resulted in flash flooding and debris flows. One particular storm even produced a landspout tornado in southeast Utah. Frequent lightning also resulted in lightning damage in Montrose, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved through the Durango east area producing hail from half inch to a little over quarter size.

Officials were notified about the rock slide just after 7:30 a.m. MDT that morning. The rock slide happened near mile post 486 just outside Needleton, which is about 55 minutes south of Silverton by train.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong to severe thunderstorm tracked down the Colorado National Monument, across the Redlands, portions of downtown Grand Junction and over the Grand Junction Regional Airport. The GJT ASOS at the Grand Junction Regional Airport recorded 1.07 inches of rain with rainfall rates of 1 inch in 30 minutes and there were several reports of hail/accumulation of hail. Heavy rain resulted in 6 to 8 inches of water running over some downtown Grand Junction streets, in parking lots and topping the curbs. There was standing water along River Road with lots of small hail contributing to drainage issues. Leach Creek near Catalina Drive filled with debris once water reached the HOA, resulting in flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and strong shear remained across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado as a positively tilted trough moved through northwest Colorado with drier air pushing in behind this wave. Instability in the 500 to 1000 J/kg range along with strong shear and enough moisture resulted in some strong to severe storms, one of which produced large hail in Moffatt County in addition to heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occurred at the Browns Park National Wildlife Refuge Headquarters location. Small hail mixed with heavy rain fell initially until larger stones fell up to golf ball size. Vehicles have damage, garden destroyed and chicken coop is full of holes. Accumulation of hail plugged up some of the culverts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering sub-tropical moisture remained in place with drier air moving in from the west as a low pressure trough passed though. Sufficient shear and enough moisture remained in place to allow for a few strong storms, one of which produced heavy rain rates over Gunnison County to result in debris flows in an area of steep terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain over the Crystal River Drainage, which caused a mudslide on Highway 133 between mile markers 45 and 46 to the west of Marble, Colorado. Between 0.3 and 0.8 inches of rain was estimated on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very deep tropical moisture associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Alberto combined with monsoonal moisture was forced through eastern Utah and western Colorado due to a large high pressure ridge over the Central U.S. and a deep low pressure trough over the Great Basin. This deep moisture combined with strong instability and shear to produce widespread severe weather across eastern Utah and western Colorado with thunderstorms producing severe wind gusts, large hail and heavy rain which resulted in flash flooding and debris flows. One particular storm even produced a landspout tornado in southeast Utah. Frequent lightning also resulted in lightning damage in Montrose, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that moved through Craig on the evening of the 21st produced several severe weather types, including winds. The peak gust was 66 mph, which was recorded at the KCAG ASOS at Craig-Moffat County Airport. In addition, at the nearby Loudy-Simpson Park, the Whittle the Wood festival sustained damage in the form of porta-potties and tents being blown over. In addition, multiple reports of downed limbs were received, some with a diameter of 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough dropped into the Great Basin, bringing well above normal moisture to eastern Utah and western Colorado in the realm of 200 to 300 percent above normal. Cold air associated with this storm system in addition to the moisture and strong winds from the upper level jet, led to widespread moderate to heavy snowfall across the mountains of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The heaviest snowfall occurred over the mountains of southwest and west-central Colorado where 1 to 2 feet of snow fell. Winds gusted upwards of 45 to 65 mph on average across most mountain ranges, with peak wind gusts of 80 to 90 mph over the San Juan mountains leading to blowing snow and whiteout conditions. Snow levels remained high with lower valleys receiving primarily rain with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 14 inches of snow fell across the area with 14 inches at Columbine Pass and 10.3 inches at Ridgway 3.6 NW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system dropped southward across eastern Utah from the Intermountain West, bringing significant snowfall to the eastern Uinta mountains before the system stalled near the Four Corners over northern Arizona. This allowed significant moisture to keep heavy snowfall ongoing over the San Juan Mountains for a few days before the low pressure system lifted northeast across west-central and northwest Colorado, bringing some additional snowfall to the northern and central mountains. The San Juan Mountains received the most snowfall due to the nearly stationary nature of this system with amounts upwards of 2 to 3 feet. Winds were also particularly strong over the southern mountains due to the presence of a strong upper level jet. This was the first significant winter storm of the season after a period of above normal warmth for early to mid October. Milder temperatures though kept snow levels above 8000 feet with most lower valleys seeing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 11 inches of snow fell across the area with upwards of 11 inches at Tower and Rabbit Ears Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As skies gradually began to clear in the wake of an upper level low, and the atmosphere began to dry out, conditions were favorable for temperatures to drop below freezing in many locations across eastern Utah and western Colorado. Northern and central Colorado in particular were favored to see freezes. Lingering moisture and cloud cover in the vicinity did limit how cold temperatures could get, however, with this being a freeze but not a season ending one.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As 7 of the 11 reporting stations available for verification in this zone reported temperatures at or below 32F for at least three hours, at least 50% of the zone was below freezing for the required length of time. However, only 2 of the 11 reported temperatures below 28F for at least 3 hours, marking this as a freeze but not a season-ending one. The minimum recorded temperature was 26F, with a range of 26-32F. The coldest temperature recorded at the KSBS AWOS in Steamboat Springs was 29F. Temperatures at or below 32F lasted on average for 5 hours, with a maximum of 9 hours and a minimum of 0 Temperatures at or below 28F lasted on average 1 hour, with a maximum of 3 hours and a minimum of 0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved through on the afternoon of the October 20th, bringing mountain snow and some colder air in its wake. Areas around the Four Corners and the adjacent southwest Colorado valleys cleared out the fasted in the wake of this low, allowing for good radiational cooling on the night of the 20th into the 21st, resulting in a freeze for some areas, though not season ending.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six of the seven stations available for verification purposes recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least three hours on the night of October 20th to the 21st. Th lowest recorded temperature was 30F, while the lowest recorded temperature from a RAWS station was 31F. Freezing temperatures lasted on average 5 hours, with the maximum being 11 hours and the minimum being 0 hours. No temperatures below 28F were recorded for any length of time, making this not a season ending freeze.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved into the mountains after midnight on the 21st and continued through much of the morning. The heaviest snowfall amounts recorded were at the ski areas where 8 to 11 inches of snow fell. The snowfall also accumulated on roadways, causing temporary closure of Interstate 70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow moved into the central Colorado mountains just after midnight on October 21st with most of the occurring before 9am. The heaviest snowfall was at the ski resorts of Summit County where 8 to 11 inches fell. It was cold enough for snow to reach the valleys where 2 to 5 inches of snow occurred. The biggest impact was brief closure of I-70 and also Loveland Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front ushered in a shallow pool of cold air to the northwestern Colorado valleys on the afternoon and evening of October 24th. As skies began to clear in the wake of the front, radiational cooling kicked in, helping the already cold temperatures to drop even further. Under this regime, two of three zones under a Freeze Warning dropped below 28F long enough to experience a season ending freeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As four of the nine reporting stations available for verification recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, at least 50% of the zone experienced a freeze. Only two of the nine stations recorded temperatures at or below 28F for at least 3 hours, so this was not a season ending freeze for the zone. The lowest temperature recorded was 22F, while the lowest recorded at the K4V0 AWOS in Rangely was 29F. Minimum temperatures ranged from 22F to 32F. Temperatures at or below 32F lasted for on average 3 hours, with a maximum of 10 hours and a minimum of 0. Temperatures at or below 28F lasted for on average 1 hour, with a maximum of 5 hours and a minimum of 0.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and cold air advection in the wake a strong late fall storm brough sub-freezing temperatures to many lower valley locations on the night of October 30th into the 31st. This event brought a season ending freeze to several locations, while a few others squeaked through with just a general freeze event. The central valleys of eastern Utah and western Colorado were most affecting, being some of the last locations still to not have had a season-ending freeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten of the eleven reporting sites available for verification recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, with six of the eleven recording temperatures at or below 28F for at least 3 hours. This means that at least 50% of the zone experienced a season-ending freeze. The minimum temperature recorded was 23F, while the minimum at the KMTJ ASOS in Montrose was 28F. Minimums ranged from 23F to 32F. Temperatures at or below 32F last for on average 10 hours, with a maximum of 19 hours and a minimum of 0. Temperatures at or below 28F lasted for on average 4.5 hours, with a maximum of 11 hours and a minimum of 0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front ushered in a shallow pool of cold air to the northwestern Colorado valleys on the afternoon and evening of October 24th. As skies began to clear in the wake of the front, radiational cooling kicked in, helping the already cold temperatures to drop even further. Under this regime, two of three zones under a Freeze Warning dropped below 28F long enough to experience a season ending freeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All eleven of the reporting stations available for verification recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, and eight of those eleven recorded temperatures at or below 28F for at least 3 hours. This means that at least 50% of the zone experienced a season-ending freeze. The minimum temperature recorded was 19F, while the minimum at the KSBS AWOS in Steamboat Springs was 25F. Minimum temperatures ranged from 19F to 32F. Temperatures at or below 32F lasted on average 11 hours, with a maximum of 13 hours and a minimum of 6 hours. Temperatures at or below 28F lasted on average 7 hours with a maximum of 12 hours and a minimum of 1.

CZ_NAME: PARADOX VALLEY / LOWER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and cold winter system began impacting the area on October 28th, bringing mountain snow and valley rain to eastern Utah and western Colorado. The cold air behind this system led to an overall cold night across eastern Utah and western Colorado, with portions of Southeast Utah and Southwest Colorado seeing lows drop below freezing under clearing skies and strong radiational cooling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five of the seven reporting stations available for verification recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, with only one station recording temperatures at or below 28F for at least 3 hours. This means at least 50% of the zone experienced a freeze, but not a season-ending freeze. The minimum temperature recorded was 26F, which was also the minimum recorded at the Carpenter Ridge RAWS site. Minimums ranged from 26F to 32F. Temperatures at or below 32F lasted for on average 10 hours, with a maximum of 19 hours and a minimum of 1 hour. Temperatures at or below 28F lasted for on average 2.5 hours, with a maximum of 16 hours and a minimum of 0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system approaching eastern Utah and western Colorado from the west drove very strong southwesterly winds out ahead of it. These winds gusted 80-85 mph over the higher terrain, and 60-65 mph across some of the valley locations. As the system moved overhead and precipitation began, winds diminished on the night of October 29th into the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust of 85 mph was recorded at the Monarch Pass AWOS station on the western side of Monarch Pass. Another gust to 72 mph was recorded at this location, along with another gust to near 50 mph at another site in the zone. Gusts ranged from 50 to 85 mph. No reports of damages were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest recorded gust was to 80 mph, recorded at the Douglas Pass Colorado Department of Transportation mesonet station. This was also the only gust recorded above the threshold. However, based on other locations of similar terrain on this day, gusts likely ranged from 50 mph to 80 mph during this period. No reports of damages were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample monsoonal moisture was in place on the afternoon of August 10, along with some modest upper level support in the form of passing shortwaves and an upper level jet. This scenario supported widespread strong to severe convection across the region, resulting in severe wind gusts as well as heavy rain that led to multiple debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite the saturated atmospheric profile, the strong to severe storms that developed on the afternoon of August 10 that were capable of producing severe winds. The peak gust with this storm was 59 mph, recorded twice at KDRO ASOS at the Durango-La Plata County Airport. This storm also produced heavy rain. No reports of damages or injuries were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a mid-level monsoonal moisture plume already in place, and a passing shortwave trough in the upper levels, a few strong to severe storms developed over portions of the plains from the 12th thru 13th of August, and also resulted in some heavy amounts of rain over El Paso County. A wet microburst from a thunderstorm also produced wind damage around the city of Pueblo due to brief gusty winds being greater than hurricane force.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported thunderstorm wind damage. Branches snapped on some smaller trees, and one window broken on the front of a convenience store.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoonal moisture remained in place with convection ongoing during the morning hours. This led to a later start to convection on the afternoon of August 11th but precipitable water values climbed to over an inch as deep monsoonal moisture continued to increase from the south, combined with a shortwave trough and sufficient lift from an upper level jet helped provide support for stronger storms towards the late afternoon and evening that led to 1 to 2 inch per hour rainfall rates on top of already saturated soils leading to significant debris flows and flash flooding, especially across southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains resulted in erosion of the Highway 141 bridge at mile marker 85.6 over Mesa Creek, roughly 8 miles northwest of Uravan. CDOT closed Highway 141 in both directions on August 12 at 1010 MST, nearly 24 to 36 hours after the inciting debris flow and scouring due to the compromised bridge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Steady heavy rains during the evening of August 11th resulted in multiple mudslides and flooding overnight in east Telluride, Bridal Veil Falls Area and Tomboy Road. This resulted in closures of several nearby passes including from the East Spur at the Town of Telluride entrance east to Black Bear, Black Bear Pass itself, Tomboy Road and Imogene Pass. Time of event was based on radar estimate.

Daily spot forecasts were requested by emergency management for both Ridgway and Ouray for extensive cleanup efforts for several days following the debris flows.

This led to a later start to convection on the afternoon of August 11th but precipitable water values climbed to over an inch as deep monsoonal moisture continued to increase from the south, combined with a shortwave trough and sufficient lift from an upper level jet helped provide support for stronger storms towards the late afternoon and evening that led to 1 to 2 inch per hour rainfall rates on top of already saturated soils leading to significant debris flows and flash flooding, especially across southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in mudslides along Highway 550 at mile marker 101.5, approximately 2 miles south-southeast of Ridgway. About an inch of storm total rainfall was reported in Ridgway with this event. Daily spot forecasts were requested by emergency management for both Ridgway and Ouray for extensive cleanup efforts for several days following the debris flows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a mid-level monsoonal moisture plume already in place, and a passing shortwave trough in the upper levels, a few strong to severe storms developed over portions of the plains from the 12th thru 13th of August, and also resulted in some heavy amounts of rain over El Paso County. A wet microburst from a thunderstorm also produced wind damage around the city of Pueblo due to brief gusty winds being greater than hurricane force.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP Observer reported hail of .88 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped southward across northwest Colorado and combined with favorable orographics and a shortwave disturbance to bring moderate snow to the Elkhead and Park Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with 12 inches at Tower. Winds gusted up to 46 mph at Storm Peak Lab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of Atmospheric Rivers following Christmas Day brought moderate to heavy snowfall over the Elkhead and Park mountains while the rest of the mountain ranges saw much lighter accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 11 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amount up to 18 inches at Tower. Winds gusted up to 53 mph at Storm Peak Lab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a brief break, another shortwave trough of low pressure moved across the area in westerly flow, carrying another increase in Atmospheric River moisture. This led to more widespread snowfall with all western Colorado mountain ranges seeing light to moderate snowfall amounts. Some higher valley locations received light snowfall amounts with lower valleys seeing rain due to milder temperatures. Winds increased during the overnight period, leading to blowing snow in the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with 12 inches at Whiskey Park. Winds gusted up to 48 mph at Storm Peak Lab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following another brief lull, a stronger wave carried a more substantial plume of Atmospheric River moisture in the 95th percentile northward allowing for heavy mountain snowfall of well over a foot to favor the Elkhead and Park Mountains in northwest Colorado. Some central Colorado mountains also saw light to moderate amounts with little snowfall across the southern mountains. Milder air continued to limit snowfall to the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amount of 31 inches of snow at Tower. Winds gusted up to 46 mph at Storm Peak Lab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After another break in the Atmospheric River train, a low pressure trough dropped in from the northwest with a strong cold front allowing snow to transition from a heavy wet snow to a drier dendritic snow. This resulted in light to moderate snowfall across the northwest Colorado mountains down to the Elk Mountains and Vail Pass and strong winds. Little to no snow occurred in the valleys with lighter amounts across the central and southern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amount of 13 inches at Tower. Wind gusts ranged from 45 to 55 mph on average with a peak wind gust of 62 mph at Storm Peak Lab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unsettled conditions at the end of December brought widespread precipitation to the region. This precipitation, in the form of both rain and snow, allowed for pockets of rich low level moisture to settle into valley locations. As skies cleared in the wake of each passing upper level wave, this rich moisture condensed into areas of dense fog, with several locations reporting visibilities of a quarter mile or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The K4V0 AWOS in Rangely, CO reported visibilities of less than 1/2 mile beginning around 6 AM local time. In addition, satellite imagery indicated widespread areas of dense fog similar to that around Rangely throughout the Lower Yampa River Basin. As the sun began to rise, a camera located at Rangely Airport, provided by the Federal Aviation Administration, as well as road cameras provided by the Colorado Department of Transportation, allowed for visual confirmation of dense fog. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary dropped from north to south through the Grand Junction County Warning Area, triggering snow showers over the northern and central Colorado Divide mountains. In addition to bringing snowfall totals of 4-8 inches across the region, this system also brought some intense wind gusts on the night of the 29th into the morning of the 30th. The strongest wind gusts were at elevations above 10000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 11:15 PM MST, the KMYP AWOS located on the western side of Monarch Pass in Colorado recorded a wind gust to 84 mph. Wind gusts around the region at elevations above about 9000 feet ranged from 60 mph to the peak gust of 84 mph at Monarch Pass. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unsettled pattern that characterized the last week of the month of December brought widespread precipitation to eastern Utah and western Colorado with regularity. This precipitation created pools of rich low level moisture trapped in the lower elevation valleys across the region. As skies cleared and temperatures cooled each night, dense fog then developed in each of these valleys, often widespread. Thanks to the low sun angle, this fog would linger well into the daylight hours before finally lifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 3AM local time, visibility at the K4V0 AWOS at the Rangely Airport, in Rangely, CO, dropped below 1/4 mile. Satellite imagery indicated that this dense fog was more than localized. As the sun rose, airfield cameras provided by the Federal Aviation Administration, and road cameras provided by the Colorado Department of Transportation added visual confirmation of the extent of the dense fog. This fog lingered well into the daylight hours thanks to a combination of already low sun angle and high clouds preventing what little sun there was from reaching the top of the fog layer. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unsettled pattern that characterized the last week of the month of December brought widespread precipitation to eastern Utah and western Colorado with regularity. This precipitation created pools of rich low level moisture trapped in the lower elevation valleys across the region. As skies cleared and temperatures cooled each night, dense fog then developed in each of these valleys, often widespread. Thanks to the low sun angle, this fog would linger well into the daylight hours before finally lifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 3AM local time, the KMTJ ASOS at the Montrose Regional Airport began reporting visibilities of 1/4 mile or less. Additionally, satellite imagery indicated that the dense fog was more than localized. As the sun rose, airfield cameras provided by the Federal Aviation Administration and road cameras provided by the Colorado Department of Transportation allowed for visual confirmation of the extent of the dense fog. Additionally, these cameras allowed the forecaster the ability to gauge when the fog was beginning to lift, which didn't occur until 12PM local time. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mountain wave brought strong west to northwest winds to the Front Range Mountains and Foothills late December 18th and into the early morning hours of December 19th. Numerous wind gusts of 65 to 85 mph were reported with few gusts topping 90 mph. A location west of Boulder (near Wallstreet) reported a peak wind gust of 102 mph. Excel energy reported 5,000-6000 customers without power. The Clear Creek County Sheriff's Office said all of Clear Creek County was without power this morning. In addition to this, a small fire was reported near Horsetooth Reservoir west of Fort Collins. Evacuations were briefly in place before the fire was quickly contained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving and complex storm system affected Colorado from November 5th through November 9th. In some locations of east central and southeast Colorado, historic amounts of snow fell. Snow began to fall on the evening of November 5th, with periods of moderate to heavy snow then affecting the region through the early morning hours of November 9th.||There were several waves of heavier snowfall, the first wave impacting the Southern Front Range Foothills, Palmer Divide, and adjacent plains through Lincoln and Washington Counties from the evening of the 5th through most of the 6th. Denver metro was on the northern edge of this first round, but temperatures were cold enough for some travel impacts on the morning of the 6th. After a brief lull on the evening of the 6th, another round of heavy snow occurred across the Palmer Divide area and adjacent plains, including Lincoln County and the I-70 Corridor east of Denver in the early morning and daylight hours of November 7th. Interstate 70 and numerous highways in east central and southeast Colorado were closed for an extended period of time due to this prolonged period of heavy snow. Rural roads were blocked by the depth of the snow, as snow had already accumulated to nearly 2 feet deep. And the storm wasn't done yet, and in fact in many cases the heaviest snow was yet to fall with the final wave of the storm.||This last round of heavy snow developed in the morning hours of the 8th, and continued through the early morning hours of the 9th. Additional accumulations during this period topped a foot of snow over the far southeast portions of Denver Metro through much of rural Arapahoe, eastern Douglas, and Elbert Counties. This round of snow was also very heavy and wet, and resulted in scattered power outages. Around 30,000 customers were without power in the Denver metro area on the morning of the 9th. Meanwhile, major interstates (I-70 east of Denver) and most highways remained closed in east central Colorado. It took days to open some rural roads.||Storm total snowfall amounts from 24 to 36 fell across the southern Foothills, Palmer Divide and across the east central plains. Local amounts exceeding 40 fell across portions of Elbert and northern Lincoln Counties, where this storm recorded the second largest snowfall totals in recorded history, second only to the early November, 1946 Snowstorm. Snowfall amounts over the Denver Metro area ranged from 12 to 24 with the highest amounts over the eastern and southern suburbs, but some melting occurred during this 90 hour storm event. Only a few inches of slushy wet snow fell farther north, including Longmont, Fort Collins, Greeley, Fort Morgan, and Sterling.||Governor Jared Polis declared a disaster emergency and activated members of the Colorado National Guard as several drivers became stranded on the Eastern Plains.|The Colorado Office of Emergency Management said crews have went on a total 22 search and rescue missions on the Eastern Plains, bringing stranded drivers to safety. Xcel Energy said there were approximately 17,600 customers without power on the morning of November 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 2 to 9 inches created slick and snow covered roads. Here are some snowfall reports, 9.0 inches Allens Park 1.5 ESE, 7.6 inches Virginia Dale 7.2 SSW, 7.5 inches Nederland 3.7 ENE, and 4.6 inches Estes Park 1.8 S.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several winter storms passed through eastern Utah and western Colorado during the last week of 2024, bringing widespread precipitation in the form of rain and snow to the region. Each system reinforced areas of rich low level moisture trapped in lower elevation valleys across western Colorado. With colder temperatures and clearing skies in the wake of each wave, the rich low level moisture condensed in dense fog that lingered well into the daylight hours before finally lifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog began to develop before 3AM local time, with the KGJT ASOS at the Grand Junction Regional Airport indicating visibilities less than 1/4 mile. Satellite imagery also indicated areas of dense low level fog throughout the Grand Valley. As the sun began to rise, road cameras provided by the Colorado Department of Transportation allowed for visual confirmation of the extent of the dense fog. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary dropped from north to south through the Grand Junction County Warning Area, triggering snow showers over the northern and central Colorado Divide mountains. In addition to bringing snowfall totals of 4-8 inches across the region, this system also brought some intense wind gusts on the night of the 29th into the morning of the 30th. The strongest wind gusts were at elevations above 10000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 6:00 AM on the morning of the 30th, the Jan P-Carbonate Hill (GBAC) site CACBH recorded a wind gust to 81 mph. This is consistent with other high winds recorded during this event, where the location was above 10000 feet in the central Colorado mountains. Wind gusts across the region ranged from 60 mph to the peak gust of 84 mph, recorded at the nearby KMYP AWOS on the western side of Monarch Pass. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received in association with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a brief break, another shortwave trough of low pressure moved across the area in westerly flow, carrying another increase in Atmospheric River moisture. This led to more widespread snowfall with all western Colorado mountain ranges seeing light to moderate snowfall amounts. Some higher valley locations received light snowfall amounts with lower valleys seeing rain due to milder temperatures. Winds increased during the overnight period, leading to blowing snow in the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the higher elevations of the area with about 9.6 inches at Columbine Pass.
