Here are a few of the stronger gust reports, 78 mph SE of Winter Park on a ridgetop, 72 mph 3 SE Tolland (Dakota Hill AWOS), and 69 mph Berthoud Pass (Berthoud Pass AWOS). No damage reported. High Wind Warning Criteria is 75 mph for this location.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved into the mountains November 3rd, becoming heavy in isolated locations. The snow continued overnight in the mountains and then spread onto the Front Range foothills and the Urban Corridor. Across the Urban Corridor, some of the precipitation fell as rain. The heaviest snowfall occurred in the Copper Mountain and Breckenridge area where an estimated 18-24 inches of snow fell, causing I-70 to closed at times in Summit County. The snow stake at Copper Mountain Ski Resort goes to 18 inches and the webcam showed the snowfall was well above the 18 inches. However, most mountain locations received much less snowfall with generally 5 to 10 inches occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowtel reports ranged from 3 to 7 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snow of developed during the morning hours on December 9th over the Palmer Divide. Even though snowfall was light, pavement and air temperatures were the right combination for snowfall to melt on paved surfaces and then freeze. This produced very slippery roads, which resulting car accidents and long traffic delays. A more organized area of snow fell during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 9th. Again the snow melted and then froze to the roadways creating very slippery conditions. Between 5 p.m. Monday and 9 a.m. Tuesday, Denver Police responded to 150 crashes ��� nearly triple the 57 crashes reported during the same timeframe earlier this week when the roads were dry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 1 to 4 inches of snow fell across the area. Here are some received reports, Sedalia 6.8 S 3.5 inches, Castle Rock 8.6 SSE 3.2 inches, and  Castle Pines 2.8 ENE 1.8 inches. I-25 became congested with traffic due to accidents and slow travel during the morning and afternoon peak traffic times.

Snow moved into the central and north central mountains early morning on the 26th. Snow persisted through early afternoon on the 27th. Total snowfall for the mountains were generally range 18-24 inches. A weak trough then brought snowfall east of the mountains Tuesday night and Wednesday morning. The foothills and Palmer Divide areas saw the heaviest snow from this, with 3 to 3 inches of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel observations showed 23.4 fell at Columbine, 20.8 inches fell at Zirkel, and 11.7 inches fell at the Buffalo Park.

The fire was quickly contained with no damage reported. Here are some wind reports from the area, 83 mph Owl Canyon 2 SW, 77 mph Buckeye, 71 mph 4 NNE Buckeye, 71 mph Christman Air Field, and 63 mph I-25 and Buckeye Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow aloft transported a plume of Pacific moisture in the Central Rockies, producing heavy mountain snow across Colorado. Loveland Pass was also closed during the storm.

The Yampa River Basin, however, received some light to moderate snowfall accumulations as the cold front dropped southward over northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area with 4.5 inches of snow reported at Vail 0.9 WNW. Winds generally gusted in the 45 to 60 mph range at elevations above 11,000 feet with a peak wind gust of 68 mph at Snowmass Ski Area Baldy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow aloft transported a plume of Pacific moisture in the Central Rockies, producing heavy mountain snow across Colorado. Snow moved into the central and north central mountains early morning on the 26th. Snow persisted through early afternoon on the 27th. Total snowfall for the mountains were generally range 18-24 inches. A weak trough then brought snowfall east of the mountains Tuesday night and Wednesday morning. The foothills and Palmer Divide areas saw the heaviest snow from this, with 3 to 3 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong Atmospheric River event brought prolonged period of heavy snowfall accumulations to southeast Utah and much of western Colorado in the days leading up to Thanksgiving, resulting in upwards of 2 to 3 feet of snow for many mountain areas with locally higher amounts above 40 inches. Some higher valley locations, in particular the Gunnison Basin and Upper Yampa River Basin, received over a foot of snow. Some areas along Interstate 70 also received moderate to heavy snowfall, including valley locations. Travel was significantly impacted during this event in the days leading up to Thanksgiving with road closures. Some lower valleys received over an inch of rain as well with light snow accumulations once the rain changed over to snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amount of 29 inches of snow reported at Cedaredge 10.5 NNW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system and associated cold front moved through the area from the Pacific Northwest, bringing light to moderate snowfall accumulations to most mountain areas of western Colorado as well as southeast Utah with roughly 4 to 10 inches. Heavy snow bands ahead of and along the cold front contributed to some locally higher amounts in some areas. The low pressure system then tracked over the southern half of the area, resulting in heavier snowfall accumulations in the San Juan Mountains upwards of 1 to 2 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with 10.5 inches at Rabbit Ears Pass. Peak wind gust of 44 mph at Storm Peak Lab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system and associated cold front moved through the area from the Pacific Northwest, bringing light to moderate snowfall accumulations to most mountain areas of western Colorado as well as southeast Utah with roughly 4 to 10 inches. Heavy snow bands ahead of and along the cold front contributed to some locally higher amounts in some areas. The low pressure system then tracked over the southern half of the area, resulting in heavier snowfall accumulations in the San Juan Mountains upwards of 1 to 2 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 13 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amount of 18 inches at Porphyry Creek. Winds gusted in the 50 to 60 mph range at elevations above 12,000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heavy, wet snow event occurred with snow starting during the evening hours on the 5th and continuing through the early morning hours of November 9th. The heaviest snow fell November 6th and November 8th with a period of lighter but still accumulating snow on the 7th. Numerous snow reports of 1-2+ feet were reported across Yuma, Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties with the highest totals across western portions of each county. Interstate 70 was shutdown initially on the 6th before reopening that night and then shutting down again on the 7th and then lasting into the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report in the town of Kit Carson reported 12 inches of snowfall for the duration of the event, along with four inches of precipitation in the rain gauge. Additional reported snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 5 inches, with 5 inches of snowfall falling from the start of the event through at least 9:17am MST 15.6 N of Haswell. Another report of 4 inches of snow fell on November 8th alone 14 SSW of Wild Horse.  Key impacts: heavy snow and visibility reduction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong Atmospheric River event brought prolonged period of heavy snowfall accumulations to southeast Utah and much of western Colorado in the days leading up to Thanksgiving, resulting in upwards of 2 to 3 feet of snow for many mountain areas with locally higher amounts above 40 inches. Some higher valley locations, in particular the Gunnison Basin and Upper Yampa River Basin, received over a foot of snow. Some areas along Interstate 70 also received moderate to heavy snowfall, including valley locations. Travel was significantly impacted during this event in the days leading up to Thanksgiving with road closures. Some lower valleys received over an inch of rain as well with light snow accumulations once the rain changed over to snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 20 to 30 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amount of 38.4 inches at Spud Mountain. 8.1 inches of snow was reported at Rockwood 6.9 NNW. Winds generally gusted in the 45 to 55 mph range at elevations above 12,000 feet with a peak wind gust of 70 mph at Eagle on 11/26.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The greatest storm total for this event was 26.9 inches 13 SW Joes in northern Kit Carson county. Additional snow reports in excess of 2 feet were reported in Flagler, 15 S Stratton and 9 SSW Joes. Additional amounts ranging from 6 to 18 inches of storm total snow was also reported across the county. There was a report 1 WSW of Burlington of a partially collapsed car port, tree limbs broken from the heavy snow as well. Interstate 70, and nearly all other highways, were closed at some point during the event. Interstate 70 first closed during the day of the 6th and then reopened that night and then closed again this time for a prolonged period from the morning of the 8th through the afternoon of the 9th. Key impacts: heavy snow, visibility reduction, and road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As moisture began to increase ahead of an atmospheric river fueled winter storm, areas of dense fog developed along the central and upper Yampa River basin in northwestern Colorado. While impacts were minimal, this fog was widespread and long-lasting, reducing visibility along the US Highway 40 corridor to less than 1/4 miles in places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread fog was spotted across the upper and central Yampa river valley via a combination of Colorado Department of Transportation cameras, satellite data, and ASOS and AWOS visibilities. Visibility at the KCAG ASOS at Craig-Moffat County Airport was reduced to 1/4 mile or less for several hours. No reports of damages or injuries were received as a result of fog on this morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, moist winter storm had moved through eastern Utah and western Colorado in the days prior to this dense fog event. Accumulating snow had made it down the floor of the Grand Valley, particularly in areas west of Fruita toward the Utah border. With warm air advection aloft and moisture and cold surface temperatures, the development of dense fog was favored on the morning of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patchy dense fog developed through the morning of November 27th across the Grand Valley, mainly west of Grand Junction and into eastern Utah along I-70. The KGJT ASOS at the Grand Junction Regional Airport reported visibility of 1/4 mile during this event. Additionally, Colorado Department of Transportation cameras along I-70 showed areas of dense fog with less than a 1/4 mile visibility in areas around the Rabbit Valley exit, and satellite imagery in combination with Utah Department of Transportation cameras indicated areas of dense fog stretched as far west at Cisco, UT. No reports of damages or injuries were received in conjunction with this fog event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An open wave trough quickly moved across the northern half of western Colorado, producing moderate snowfall accumulations for the Park Mountains and Flattop Mountains with wind gusts exceeding 45 mph over Storm Peak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 10 inches with locally higher amount of 18 inches at Tower. Peak wind gust of 53 mph was recorded at Storm Peak Lab on 11/12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a convergence boundary that stretched from Limon to Sterling. The storms produce hail up to an inch and a half in diameter, winds to 60 mph, two landspout tornadoes and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple spotters have confirmed location. Tornado lasted about 5-6 minutes. It remained nearly stationary for about 2 minutes, then slowly drifted westward.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of the 20th, a cluster of storms began to from in north central Colorado and moved east. As the evening progressed, this cluster grew into more of a line. This line of storms produced up to four inch diameter hail, accumulating hail up to a foot deep, 75 MPH wind gusts, and heavy rain.  The combined effect of accumulating hail and heavy rainfall led to street and basement flooding in Yuma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report and photos via social media of accumulating hail and hail drifts in Yuma. Biggest stones baseball size or larger with most stones 1 to 2.25 inch. Trained spotters in Yuma also reported hail up to around 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt runoff in the upper Arkansas (above Pueblo) and high flow releases from Pueblo Reservoir resulted in minor flooding at numerous forecast points along the Arkansas River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained high flow releases from Pueblo Reservoir resulted in minor river flooding along the Arkansas River near Avondale, CO (ADLC2). The ADLC2 stream gauge recorded a crest of 8.97 ft, occurring on June 10, and remained above Minor Flood Stage until June 17. Minor inundation of low-lying areas and riparian zone occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moving over the region produced two days of severe storms, which resulted in winds of severe criteria at both the Pueblo and Lamar airports, and also some wind damage in Canon City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public source (via mPing) reported wind damage of 1 inch tree limbs and shingles blown off roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An existing boundary parked over the lower Arkansas River Valley produced several severe storms over Fremont County, which resulted in hail of severe criteria, and brief heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public source reported hail of 1.25 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An embedded wave was transitioning over the region, resulting in the development of stronger storms, which produced heavy rain. Thunderstorms producing heavy rainfall resulted in flooded roadways in some areas in Pueblo, CO.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain occurred from a thunderstorm, with a measured amount of 1.77 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary stalled out over the plains and resulted in the development of strong storms. Heavy rainfall from thunderstorms produced some flash flooding in Huerfano and Pueblo Counties. Isolated stronger storms (sub-severe) also developed further west over Saguache County, resulting in some hail (below severe criteria) and a brief landspout was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall led to some water over short stretches of Hwy 160, county roads, ditches, and other low-lying areas. No other notable impacts reported. Radar-estimated rainfall of 1.5-2.0 inches occurred over impacted areas within warning area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Arkansas River in Ca��on City (CNCC2) fluctuated just above and below Minor Flood Stage for a couple days due to a combination of accelerated snowmelt runoff in the upper Arkansas River Basin and from sustained high flow releases from Turquoise Lake and Twin Lakes. The CNCC2 stream gauge recorded crests of 10.02 ft on two occasions from June 10 through 11.

CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Alexander Mountain Fire started on Monday, July 29, 2024, north of US Highway 34 and west of Loveland, Colorado. Evacuations and road closures were in effect for several days. A total of 28 homes and 21 outbuildings lost in the fire. Final acreage of the fire has been mapped at 9,668 acres. US Highway 34 between Loveland and Estes Park fully reopened on August 8, 2024. All evacuation orders were lifted as of 10:48 a.m. on August 12, exactly two weeks from the time the Alexander Mountain Fire was first reported to dispatch.
